description: Writing competency ontology for VA gold-tier system: evaluator (WKE), Writing Coach, LRET, Essay Revision, Practice Engine, Learning Intelligence Engine.

macro_domains.description: Abstract reasoning capacity demonstrated in essay text: causal chains, depth of explanation, handling of alternatives, qualification, and logical coherence.

micro_skills.definition: Correctly classify the writing genre required by the prompt (e.g., argumentative essay, report, formal letter, data description, narrative).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Genre misidentification causes structural and register failures that invalidate the entire response before a single sentence is evaluated for quality.

evaluation_metrics.description: Detected genre matches target genre
Count of genre-appropriate structural signals present (e.g., salutation for letter, overview for report)

score_formula.description: Binary genre match weighted by signal density

progress_tracking.rolling_30_days: Mean score over essays in last 30 days

2_min.title: Genre Flash Cards
2_min.instruction: Read 5 prompt extracts. Label each genre. Check answers.

5_min.title: Genre Signal Hunt
5_min.instruction: Given one model essay, list every structural signal that marks its genre.

10_min.title: Wrong Genre Rewrite
10_min.instruction: Rewrite an argumentative essay opening paragraph as a formal report overview.
10_min.success_criterion: Evaluator detects correct genre signals in rewrite

15_min.title: Genre Contrast Analysis
15_min.instruction: Compare two model responses to different prompt types. Write a 100-word analysis of how genre shapes structure.
15_min.success_criterion: Analysis correctly names ≥ 3 genre-specific features per text

failure_patterns: Writing an argumentative essay when a report is required
Using informal genre markers (e.g., contractions, first-person narrative) in academic genre
Omitting required genre components (e.g., no overview in data description)
strength_indicators: First paragraph immediately establishes correct genre through structure
All required genre components present in correct positions
Register consistent with genre throughout

ielts_mapping.task_response: Primary — genre compliance is prerequisite for TR scoring

universal_writing_mapping.academic_writing: Critical — reports, essays, literature reviews each have distinct genre rules
universal_writing_mapping.university_writing: Critical — assignments specify genre; misidentification = fail
universal_writing_mapping.business_writing: High — memo vs. report vs. proposal require different forms
universal_writing_mapping.general_writing: Moderate — genre awareness improves letter and email appropriateness

micro_skills.definition: Determine the specific operation the prompt requires (e.g., describe, compare, argue, discuss both views, evaluate, explain causes, propose solutions).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Task type governs content selection. A 'causes' task answered with 'solutions' content produces zero-relevant content even if the writing is fluent.

evaluation_metrics.description: Detected task operation matches prompt-required operation
Proportion of required task components addressed

score_formula.description: Task type match multiplied by component coverage

2_min.title: Task Verb Decode
2_min.instruction: Extract the task verb(s) from 5 prompts. Map each to an operation.

5_min.title: Component Checklist
5_min.instruction: For a given prompt, list every component the response must include.

10_min.title: Task Annotation
10_min.instruction: Annotate a model essay: highlight each sentence that addresses each task component.

15_min.title: Parallel Prompt Writing
15_min.instruction: Given a 'discuss both views' prompt, write a parallel 'agree/disagree' version and a 'causes/solutions' version. Note how the required content changes.
15_min.success_criterion: Content shifts correctly match task type in both

failure_patterns: Answering only one part of a two-part prompt
Treating 'discuss both views and give your opinion' as 'agree or disagree'
Providing causes when solutions are required
strength_indicators: All prompt components addressed in correct proportion
Task verbs echoed or semantically matched in thesis and topic sentences

micro_skills.definition: Determine the intended reader of the text (e.g., examiner, academic peer, manager, general public) and adjust content density, register, and assumed knowledge accordingly.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Audience determines appropriate vocabulary level, explanation depth, formality, and what background knowledge can be assumed. Wrong audience calibration produces over-simplified or inaccessible text.

evaluation_metrics.description: Register level matches implied audience
Terms defined when audience cannot be assumed to know them; not over-explained when audience is expert

2_min.title: Audience Quick-Sort
2_min.instruction: Given 5 text extracts, identify the intended audience for each.

5_min.title: Register Swap
5_min.instruction: Rewrite one paragraph for a different audience (academic → general public).

10_min.title: Jargon Audit
10_min.instruction: Read your own essay. List every technical term. Mark which require definition for your target audience.
10_min.success_criterion: All unmarked terms are genuinely accessible to target audience

15_min.title: Dual Audience Draft
15_min.instruction: Write an introduction twice: once for academic journal readers, once for a newspaper reader. Same topic.
15_min.success_criterion: Vocabulary complexity and explanation depth differ measurably

failure_patterns: Using informal language in formal academic contexts
Over-explaining obvious concepts to expert audiences
Failing to define specialist terms for general audiences
strength_indicators: Register perfectly calibrated to stated audience
Technical terms defined on first use where required
Explanation depth consistent with audience expertise

micro_skills.definition: Determine the communicative goal of the writing task (e.g., to persuade, to inform, to analyse, to recommend, to describe).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Purpose shapes argument strategy, evidence selection, and conclusion type. A persuasive purpose requires different rhetorical moves than an analytical one.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of paragraphs whose rhetorical function aligns with stated purpose

2_min.title: Purpose Label Drill
2_min.instruction: Label the purpose of 5 prompt extracts.

5_min.title: Purpose Map
5_min.instruction: For your last essay, label each paragraph's purpose. Check alignment with overall task purpose.

10_min.title: Purpose Shift Rewrite
10_min.instruction: Take an informative paragraph and rewrite it as persuasive. Identify what changed.

15_min.title: Purpose-Driven Outline
15_min.instruction: For a given prompt, write a full paragraph-by-paragraph outline. Label the communicative purpose of each paragraph.
15_min.success_criterion: Every paragraph label is justified and consistent with overall purpose

failure_patterns: Narrating events instead of arguing a position
Switching purpose mid-essay
Ending with description instead of evaluation
strength_indicators: Every paragraph advances the stated purpose
Conclusion matches purpose (persuasive → recommendation; analytical → synthesis)

micro_skills.definition: Recognise and respect explicit and implicit limitations imposed by the prompt (word count, time frame, scope, perspective, format rules).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Constraint violations (under/over length, wrong scope) are penalised mechanically before content is assessed.

evaluation_metrics.description: Essay word count within ±10% of target
Essay does not introduce out-of-scope content

2_min.title: Constraint Extraction
2_min.instruction: Extract all constraints from 3 prompts and list them.

5_min.title: Word Budget Plan
5_min.instruction: For a 250-word task, plan word allocation per section.
5_min.success_criterion: Sections sum to 250 and proportions are reasonable

10_min.title: Scope Audit
10_min.instruction: Read an essay. Highlight every sentence that goes beyond the prompt scope.

15_min.title: Constrained Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a response to a complex prompt under strict word and scope constraints.
15_min.success_criterion: Word count ±5% of target; zero off-scope content

failure_patterns: Consistently writing under minimum word count
Introducing examples from prohibited domains
Ignoring perspective constraints (e.g., writing impersonally when first-person is required)
strength_indicators: Word count precisely calibrated
Every sentence traceable to prompt scope

micro_skills.definition: Enumerate every structural and content component the task demands before writing begins (e.g., overview + two body paragraphs for WT1; intro + position + arguments + conclusion for WT2).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Missing components are the single most common cause of band score ceiling in task response.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of required components present in essay
Components appear in structurally correct positions

2_min.title: Component Checklist Drill
2_min.instruction: For 3 prompt types, list all required components from memory.

5_min.title: Essay X-Ray
5_min.instruction: Label each paragraph of a model essay with the component it fulfils.

10_min.title: Missing Component Repair
10_min.instruction: Given a flawed essay missing one required component, write the missing section.

15_min.title: Component-First Outline
15_min.instruction: Create a complete annotated outline for a complex two-part prompt.
15_min.success_criterion: Every required component mapped to a specific paragraph

failure_patterns: Missing overview in data description
No clear opinion in opinion essay
Missing conclusion
strength_indicators: All required components present and clearly demarcated
Components proportionally developed

micro_skills.definition: Understand the criteria by which the writing will be judged and weight effort allocation accordingly (e.g., knowing that TR carries equal weight to other bands; knowing a thesis is required).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who do not understand evaluation criteria misallocate effort — they polish vocabulary while leaving critical thinking undeveloped.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of essay word count devoted to high-weight criteria areas

2_min.title: Criteria Weight Drill
2_min.instruction: Rank the four IELTS criteria by their impact on band score for a given task.

5_min.title: Score Gap Analysis
5_min.instruction: Given a scored essay, identify which criterion caused the most band loss.

10_min.title: Effort Map
10_min.instruction: Annotate your own essay by criteria. Calculate word % per criterion area.
10_min.success_criterion: Analysis reveals underinvestment in at least one criterion

15_min.title: Strategic Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite one underperforming section with explicit focus on the lowest-scoring criterion.
15_min.success_criterion: Criterion score improves in evaluator assessment

failure_patterns: Spending 80% of revision time on spelling while TR is failing
Not knowing that 'overview' is mandatory for Task 1
strength_indicators: Student can articulate what each criterion requires before writing

micro_skills.definition: Sustain relevance to the prompt throughout the entire essay without drifting into tangential content or losing the central question.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Partial task focus is the most common TR failure mode at B2 level. Even strong writers drift in body paragraphs 2–3.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of sentences directly relevant to the prompt
Decrease in relevance score from paragraph 1 to final body paragraph

2_min.title: Relevance Scanner
2_min.instruction: Read 5 sentences. Mark each as on-task or off-task for a given prompt.

5_min.title: Drift Detection
5_min.instruction: Read your last essay's body paragraphs only. Highlight every off-topic sentence.

10_min.title: Refocus Rewrite
10_min.instruction: Take a drifting paragraph and rewrite it with strict prompt relevance.

15_min.title: Focus-Locked Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a full essay body (3 paragraphs). After each paragraph, check and record its relevance to the prompt before proceeding.

failure_patterns: Opening sentence is relevant but examples stray to tangential topics
Final body paragraph introduces a new topic unrelated to the prompt
Counter-argument section drifts from the central debate
strength_indicators: Every sentence traceable to prompt keyword
Topic sentences in each paragraph reference prompt language
Conclusion restates prompt terms

micro_skills.definition: Produce ideas that are directly applicable to the prompt topic and task type, without defaulting to memorised generic content.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Relevant, specific ideas are the raw material of high task response scores. Generic or memorised content is penalised as it fails to engage with the specific prompt.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of distinct ideas that are prompt-relevant
Average specificity of ideas (generic=0, domain-specific=0.5, prompt-specific=1.0)

2_min.title: 60-Second Brainstorm
2_min.instruction: Set timer. Write as many relevant ideas as possible for a prompt in 60 seconds.

5_min.title: Idea Relevance Sort
5_min.instruction: Given 10 ideas for a prompt, sort into relevant/irrelevant. Justify each.

10_min.title: Idea Upgrade
10_min.instruction: Take 3 generic ideas ('technology is useful'). Rewrite each as a prompt-specific idea.

15_min.title: Idea Map Build
15_min.instruction: For a given prompt, create a full idea map: central topic → 3 main ideas → supporting detail for each.
15_min.success_criterion: All 3 main ideas are prompt-relevant and specific

failure_patterns: Recycling generic ideas across all prompts
Ideas that are true but unrelated to the specific prompt angle
Over-relying on a single memorised example
strength_indicators: Ideas contain prompt-specific nouns and concepts
Different prompt produces noticeably different idea set

micro_skills.definition: Produce a range of distinct ideas before writing, enabling selection of the strongest rather than defaulting to the first idea generated.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who plan with only one idea per paragraph are forced to pad with repetition. Multiple ideas enable selection and coverage.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of distinct main ideas in essay
Semantic dissimilarity between ideas (1.0 = maximally diverse)

2_min.title: Idea Sprint x3
2_min.instruction: Generate 3 distinct ideas for a prompt in 2 minutes.

5_min.title: Perspective Scan
5_min.instruction: Generate ideas from 3 different stakeholder perspectives for one topic.

10_min.title: Idea Selection Practice
10_min.instruction: Generate 6 ideas. Rank them by quality. Explain why you chose your top 2.

15_min.title: Multi-View Essay Plan
15_min.instruction: Plan a 4-paragraph essay with 2 distinct main ideas and 1 counter-idea.

failure_patterns: One idea repeated across all paragraphs with different vocabulary
Two ideas that are actually the same idea rephrased
strength_indicators: Each body paragraph introduces a genuinely new idea
Ideas span different dimensions of the topic

micro_skills.definition: Produce concrete, specific examples that illustrate abstract claims rather than restating the claim or using generic placeholders.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Unsupported claims score low on task response and reasoning. Specific examples are the primary mechanism for demonstrating depth.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of concrete examples per body paragraph
Specificity rating: 0=none, 0.33=generic, 0.66=domain-specific, 1.0=precise (named, quantified, or situated)
Proportion of examples explicitly linked back to the claim they support

2_min.title: Vague → Specific
2_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 vague examples to specific ones. ('Many countries' → name one; 'studies show' → name a finding)

5_min.title: Example-Claim Linking
5_min.instruction: For 3 given claims, write one specific example and one linking sentence that returns to the claim.
5_min.success_criterion: All linking sentences reference claim language

10_min.title: Example Bank Build
10_min.instruction: Build a personal example bank: 5 examples across 3 common topics (technology, environment, education). Each must be specific.

15_min.title: Evidence-Dense Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write one body paragraph with claim → 2 specific examples → analytical link back to claim.
15_min.success_criterion: Both examples specific; link sentence references claim

failure_patterns: 'For example, many people think...' (restates claim)
'For instance, in today's society...' (generic filler)
Example given but never linked back to claim
strength_indicators: Examples contain named entities, statistics, or situated scenarios
Every example followed by an explicit analytical link

micro_skills.definition: Unpack a claim or example by explaining the mechanism, process, or logic that makes it valid.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Raw assertions and examples without explanation produce shallow reasoning. Explanation is what moves writing from descriptive to analytical.

evaluation_metrics.description: Average number of logical steps per argument (claim→mechanism→consequence)
Proportion of claims with no explanatory follow-through

2_min.title: Because Chain
2_min.instruction: For 3 given claims, write 2 sentences that explain why the claim is true.
2_min.success_criterion: Each adds genuine logical content, not repetition

5_min.title: Mechanism Unpack
5_min.instruction: Take a claim ('Social media causes anxiety'). Write a 3-step causal chain explaining the mechanism.

10_min.title: Shallow→Deep Rewrite
10_min.instruction: Rewrite a shallow paragraph by inserting explanatory sentences after each claim and example.

15_min.title: CEE Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph using Claim → Explanation (mechanism) → Evidence (example) → Evaluation structure.
15_min.success_criterion: All 4 structural elements identifiable by evaluator

failure_patterns: Claim followed directly by another claim
Explanation that is just a restatement in different words
Jumping to examples without first explaining the principle
strength_indicators: Causal language present (because, as a result, this means that, consequently)
Explanation sentences are longer and more complex than claim sentences

micro_skills.definition: Construct meaningful comparisons between entities, time periods, groups, or scenarios to support a point or describe data.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Comparison is a primary analytical operation in both academic writing and data description. Inability to compare limits analytical depth and data summary accuracy.

evaluation_metrics.description: Comparisons are factually correct relative to data or logic
Grammatical and logical correctness of comparison structures

2_min.title: Comparison Sentence Drill
2_min.instruction: Write 3 comparison sentences from given data pairs using different structures (more...than, compared to, while).

5_min.title: Chart Comparison
5_min.instruction: Compare two data points from a given chart using 3 different comparative structures.
5_min.success_criterion: All factually accurate and structurally varied

10_min.title: Argument Comparison
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that contrasts two opposing positions on a topic using hedged language.

15_min.title: Comparative Overview
15_min.instruction: Write a 100-word overview of a given dataset using only comparison language.
15_min.success_criterion: All key comparisons present and accurate

failure_patterns: Comparing incomparable entities
Missing the object of comparison ('X is higher' — higher than what?)
Using comparison structures incorrectly (e.g., 'more higher')
strength_indicators: Comparisons always specify both terms
Variety in comparative structures
Comparisons grounded in data or evidence

micro_skills.definition: Identify and articulate causal factors behind a phenomenon with appropriate specificity and logical grounding.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Cause-effect is one of the most common IELTS and academic writing task types. Shallow cause generation (e.g., 'due to modernisation') is the primary failure mode.

evaluation_metrics.description: Average specificity of causes generated (0=vague, 1=precise mechanism)
Number of distinct causes identified
Proportion of causes logically valid for the stated phenomenon

2_min.title: Why Chain
2_min.instruction: For a given problem, ask 'why?' three times to reach a root cause.
2_min.success_criterion: Each step is logically derived from the previous

5_min.title: Cause Taxonomy
5_min.instruction: Generate 4 causes for a phenomenon across 4 categories: economic, social, technological, political.

10_min.title: Cause Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph that identifies one cause and explains its mechanism in detail.
10_min.success_criterion: Cause is specific and mechanism is explained in ≥ 2 sentences

15_min.title: Cause-Effect Essay Body
15_min.instruction: Write 2 cause paragraphs for a given problem. Each must state a different cause with a distinct mechanism.

failure_patterns: 'Because of globalisation' (no mechanism)
Restating the effect as a cause
All causes from the same category
strength_indicators: Causes name a specific mechanism or agent
Causes are logically prior to the stated effect

micro_skills.definition: Identify and articulate the effects, impacts, or outcomes of a stated cause or action with specificity.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Consequence generation is the second half of causal reasoning tasks and is also required in opinion essays to justify positions.

evaluation_metrics.description: Specificity of consequences (0=vague, 1=precise outcome with scope)
Proportion of consequences logically derivable from stated cause

2_min.title: So What? Drill
2_min.instruction: For 3 given statements, write one consequence using 'As a result / therefore / this means that'.

5_min.title: Ripple Effect
5_min.instruction: Map a chain: Cause → Immediate effect → Second-order effect.

10_min.title: Consequence Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that explains two distinct consequences of a given cause.
10_min.success_criterion: Both consequences are specific and distinct

15_min.title: Balanced Impact Essay
15_min.instruction: Write body paragraphs covering one positive and one negative consequence of a given change.
15_min.success_criterion: Both consequences specific and analytically developed

failure_patterns: 'This will have a negative effect on society' (no specification)
Circular consequence (restates the cause)
Consequence affecting an implausibly large scope
strength_indicators: Consequences name specific groups, systems, or outcomes
Consequence follows logically from mechanism, not just assertion

micro_skills.definition: Propose actionable, specific, and feasible responses to a stated problem with clear links to the causal analysis.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Problem-solution tasks require solutions that address identified causes. Generic solutions ('the government should act') score near zero on task response.

evaluation_metrics.description: Specificity of proposed solutions (0=vague, 1=named agent + action + mechanism)
Proportion of solutions that directly address an identified cause
Solution is plausible (not hyperbolic or impossible)

2_min.title: Agent + Action Drill
2_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 vague solutions by specifying agent and action. ('Governments should act' → specific)
2_min.success_criterion: All 3 include named agent + specific action

5_min.title: Cause-Solution Mapping
5_min.instruction: For 2 given causes, write 1 solution each that directly addresses that cause.
5_min.success_criterion: Each solution traces to its cause

10_min.title: Solution Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph: restate the cause → propose specific solution → explain mechanism.

15_min.title: Problem-Solution Essay Body
15_min.instruction: Write 2 solution paragraphs, each addressing a different cause from a given problem.
15_min.success_criterion: Both solutions specific, feasible, and cause-aligned

failure_patterns: Solutions that don't address identified causes
'The government should invest in education' (no mechanism)
Infeasible solutions presented without qualification
strength_indicators: Solution paragraph opens by referencing the cause it addresses
Solutions include a named agent, specific action, and expected outcome

micro_skills.definition: Produce data, research references, statistics, or documented facts to support claims, distinguishing evidence from opinion or assertion.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Evidence-backed claims are more persuasive and score higher on task response in academic and university contexts.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of claims supported by at least one evidence item
Quality of evidence: 0=none, 0.33=anecdote, 0.66=general reference, 1.0=specific data/citation

2_min.title: Evidence vs. Opinion Sort
2_min.instruction: Label 8 sentences as evidence or opinion. Justify.

5_min.title: Evidence Upgrade
5_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 opinion statements to evidence-backed claims.

10_min.title: Evidence Integration
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph where a claim is introduced, evidence is cited, and a conclusion is drawn.
10_min.success_criterion: Evidence is specific and conclusion follows from it

15_min.title: Evidence Map
15_min.instruction: Plan an essay body: for each claim, list what evidence you would use and its type.
15_min.success_criterion: Each claim has at least one evidence item mapped

failure_patterns: 'Studies have shown...' with no specifics
Using personal opinion as evidence
Citing evidence that doesn't support the claim
strength_indicators: Evidence includes specific numbers, named studies, or documented cases
Evidence is logically linked to the claim it supports

micro_skills.definition: Articulate the strongest opposing position to one's own argument fairly and specifically, without strawmanning.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Counterargument demonstrates intellectual engagement with opposing views — a core criterion in academic argumentation. Absent counterarguments signal one-dimensional thinking.

evaluation_metrics.description: At least one counterargument present in essay
Counterargument represents the strongest version of the opposing view
Counterargument misrepresents or weakens the opposing position

2_min.title: Steel Man Drill
2_min.instruction: For your last essay's main claim, write the strongest possible counter-claim in 1–2 sentences.
2_min.success_criterion: Evaluator rates it as a genuine challenge to the main claim

5_min.title: Devil's Advocate
5_min.instruction: Take a position you agree with. Write 3 reasons why a reasonable person would disagree.

10_min.title: Concession Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a concession paragraph: present a counterargument fairly, then qualify or rebut it.
10_min.success_criterion: Counterargument is fairly presented; rebuttal is logical

15_min.title: Two-Sided Essay Section
15_min.instruction: Write a 'both views' section of an essay presenting two genuinely opposing positions with equal development.
15_min.success_criterion: Both views developed with ≥ 2 sentences each

failure_patterns: Strawman: 'Some people think technology is bad because they don't understand it'
Counterargument is a weaker version of own claim
No counterargument present in a discursive essay
strength_indicators: Counterargument acknowledged before being qualified
Counterargument could stand alone as a valid point

micro_skills.definition: Formulate a clear, arguable, specific proposition that takes a position and can be supported with evidence.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A well-formed claim is the atomic unit of argument. Vague or non-arguable claims make all subsequent reasoning impossible to evaluate.

evaluation_metrics.description: Claim makes a disputable proposition (not a fact or tautology)
Claim names specific subject, condition, or scope
Proportion of body paragraphs with at least one identifiable claim

2_min.title: Claim vs. Fact Sort
2_min.instruction: Label 6 sentences as claim or fact. Justify.

5_min.title: Claim Sharpener
5_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 vague claims to specific arguable claims.

10_min.title: Claim + Justification
10_min.instruction: Write 2 claims with one sentence justifying why each is arguable.

15_min.title: Claim Hierarchy
15_min.instruction: Write a thesis claim and 2 supporting sub-claims. Show how sub-claims support the thesis.

failure_patterns: 'Technology is important in modern life' (non-arguable fact)
Topic sentence that is a question, not a claim
Paragraph with no identifiable claim — only examples
strength_indicators: Every body paragraph opens with a disputable, specific claim
Claim language is assertive rather than exploratory

micro_skills.definition: Develop arguments through multiple logical steps from claim to sub-claim to evidence to implication, rather than asserting without elaboration.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Shallow reasoning is the primary ceiling for B2 writers who have fluency but score Band 6 on TR. Depth is what separates Band 7 from Band 6.

evaluation_metrics.description: Average number of logical steps per argument chain (1=bare assertion, 3=full developed argument)
Proportion of claims followed by explanation or evidence

2_min.title: Depth Ladder
2_min.instruction: Take a 1-sentence claim. Add 2 more sentences that deepen it without repeating.
2_min.success_criterion: Each sentence adds new logical content

5_min.title: Step Counter
5_min.instruction: Read your last essay's body paragraph 1. Count the logical steps. Identify where depth ends.

10_min.title: Shallow → Deep Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Rewrite a shallow paragraph (1-step argument) to include 3 logical steps.

15_min.title: Deep Argument Draft
15_min.instruction: Write one body paragraph targeting maximum reasoning depth: claim → explanation → evidence → implication → counter-consideration.

failure_patterns: List of claims with no development
Example given but never analysed
Conclusion paragraph that introduces new arguments instead of synthesising
strength_indicators: Each paragraph has ≥ 3 logical steps
Analytical sentences outnumber assertion sentences

micro_skills.definition: Evaluate whether supporting material (examples, data, references) is logically sufficient and directly relevant to the claim it supports.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers often include evidence that is tangentially related or that proves a different point. Weak support undermines the argument even when it is verbose.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of support items directly relevant to their stated claim
Whether the volume and type of support is sufficient to make the claim convincing

2_min.title: Support Match
2_min.instruction: Match 4 support items to the 4 claims they best support from a shuffled list.

5_min.title: Weak Support Diagnosis
5_min.instruction: Read a paragraph. Identify whether the support is relevant, sufficient, or neither.

10_min.title: Support Upgrade
10_min.instruction: Replace weak or tangential support in a given paragraph with stronger, directly relevant support.
10_min.success_criterion: Support rated as relevant and sufficient

15_min.title: Claim-Support Audit
15_min.instruction: Audit your own essay. For each claim, rate support relevance and sufficiency. Revise the weakest.
15_min.success_criterion: Revised support rated stronger than original

failure_patterns: Using an example that illustrates a related but different claim
Providing one sentence of support for a complex claim
Using the same example to support two different claims
strength_indicators: Each support item directly addressed in the claim's language
Multiple forms of support (example + data + explanation)

micro_skills.definition: Embed evidence into the argument flow so that it advances the point rather than interrupting it, using appropriate framing and post-evidence commentary.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who list evidence without framing or commentary produce disconnected paragraphs. Integration is what creates coherent argumentation.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of evidence items introduced with a framing phrase
Proportion of evidence items followed by analytical commentary

2_min.title: Frame It
2_min.instruction: Add a framing phrase to 3 bare evidence sentences. ('Japan reduced emissions by 20%' → 'This is demonstrated by Japan's...')

5_min.title: Commentary Add-On
5_min.instruction: Take 3 evidence sentences. After each, write one analytical sentence explaining what the evidence proves.
5_min.success_criterion: All reference the claim, not just the evidence

10_min.title: CER Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a Claim → Evidence → Reasoning paragraph with at least 2 evidence items, each framed and followed by commentary.
10_min.success_criterion: Evaluator identifies both framing and commentary for each evidence item

15_min.title: Integration Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Take a paragraph where evidence is listed without framing. Rewrite it with full integration.

failure_patterns: Evidence dropped without introduction: 'Japan, 30% reduction. This is important.'
Evidence ends the paragraph with no commentary
Commentary that only restates the evidence
strength_indicators: Every evidence item introduced and followed by analysis
Commentary uses different vocabulary from the evidence sentence

micro_skills.definition: Construct valid causal chains in which a stated cause is logically sufficient and necessary to produce the stated effect, avoiding confounding, reverse causation, and post hoc fallacies.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Flawed causal reasoning is a primary marker of Band 5–6 writing. It undermines logical coherence regardless of fluency.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of cause-effect claims that are logically valid
Number of detected causal fallacies (post hoc, circular, false dilemma)

2_min.title: Fallacy Spotter
2_min.instruction: Label 4 causal claims as valid/fallacious. Name the fallacy if present.

5_min.title: Causal Chain Repair
5_min.instruction: Fix 2 flawed causal chains by identifying the logical error and rewriting.
5_min.success_criterion: Both chains rated valid by evaluator

10_min.title: Multi-Step Chain
10_min.instruction: Write a 4-step causal chain for a given topic. Each step must be logically necessary.

15_min.title: Causal Paragraph Critique
15_min.instruction: Annotate a model essay's causal claims. Identify valid chains and fallacies. Rewrite one flawed section.
15_min.success_criterion: All fallacies identified; rewrite rated valid

failure_patterns: 'People use social media; therefore, they are less happy' (correlation as causation)
Circular: 'Poverty causes crime because criminals are poor'
Reversing cause and effect
strength_indicators: Causal language is precise (leads to, contributes to, is a precondition for)
Causal claims are qualified where full causation cannot be established

micro_skills.definition: Assess multiple options, approaches, or positions against stated criteria to support a conclusion or recommendation.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Single-option thinking produces one-sided essays. Evaluating alternatives demonstrates analytical sophistication expected at B2+.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of distinct alternatives considered
Whether criteria for evaluation are stated or implied

2_min.title: Option Compare
2_min.instruction: For a given problem, write one advantage and one disadvantage of two different solutions.

5_min.title: Criteria Setting
5_min.instruction: For a given choice, list 3 criteria you would use to evaluate options. Justify each.

10_min.title: Comparative Evaluation Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that evaluates two alternatives against one criterion and reaches a conclusion.
10_min.success_criterion: Both alternatives discussed; conclusion supported by evaluation

15_min.title: Evaluate and Recommend
15_min.instruction: Write a short recommendation section: evaluate 2 options against 2 criteria and recommend one.

failure_patterns: Only one option considered in a task requiring evaluation
Criteria unstated — conclusion appears arbitrary
All alternatives dismissed without genuine engagement
strength_indicators: Multiple alternatives explicitly named and compared
Evaluation criteria stated before comparison begins

micro_skills.definition: Develop an opposing argument to a sufficient depth (claim + explanation + evidence) before rebutting or conceding.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Underdeveloped counterarguments are detected as strategic avoidance. Full development demonstrates fairness and intellectual confidence.

evaluation_metrics.description: Steps in the counterargument chain (1=bare claim, 3=claim+explanation+evidence)
Words devoted to counterargument as proportion of body paragraph words

2_min.title: Develop the Other Side
2_min.instruction: Take a one-sentence counterargument. Add an explanation and an example to develop it.

5_min.title: Equal Development Check
5_min.instruction: Compare word counts of your main argument and counterargument in your last essay. Identify imbalance.

10_min.title: Full Counter Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a counterargument paragraph: claim → explanation → evidence → transition to rebuttal.

15_min.title: Concession-Rebuttal Essay Section
15_min.instruction: Write a full concession-rebuttal passage (counterargument developed + qualified rebuttal).
15_min.success_criterion: Counter fully developed; rebuttal adds new logic, not repetition

failure_patterns: Counterargument dismissed in one clause: '...although some people disagree'
Counterargument developed less than main claim
Counterargument and rebuttal in the same sentence
strength_indicators: Counterargument paragraph has equivalent depth to main argument paragraph
Rebuttal appears after full development, not before

micro_skills.definition: Respond to a counterargument with a logically valid qualification, limitation, or counter-evidence that defends the main position without dismissing the opposing view.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A weak rebuttal ('However, I still believe...') destroys the analytical value of the counterargument section. Strong rebuttal is a key B2+ differentiator.

evaluation_metrics.description: Rebuttal adds new logical content beyond restating own position
Rebuttal addresses specific claims in counterargument, not the general position

2_min.title: Rebuttal Detector
2_min.instruction: Label 4 rebuttals as strong (adds new logic) or weak (repeats own view).

5_min.title: Limitation Rebuttal
5_min.instruction: For a given counterargument, write a rebuttal that concedes a limited point while defending the main claim.
5_min.success_criterion: Evaluator confirms concession + defence both present

10_min.title: Evidence Rebuttal
10_min.instruction: Write a rebuttal using counter-evidence (a specific fact that limits the counterargument).
10_min.success_criterion: Counter-evidence is specific; logic is valid

15_min.title: Full Concession-Rebuttal Sequence
15_min.instruction: Write counterargument (3 sentences) + rebuttal (3 sentences). Rebuttal must add 2 new logical elements.
15_min.success_criterion: Evaluator identifies 2 new elements in rebuttal

failure_patterns: 'However, I still maintain that...' (no new content)
Rebuttal attacks the person or motive, not the argument
Rebuttal that fully concedes the counterargument without defending main claim
strength_indicators: Rebuttal contains a qualifier ('while this may be true in X context', 'this applies only to...')
Rebuttal introduces new evidence not used elsewhere

micro_skills.definition: Appropriately limit the scope of claims using hedging, conditionals, and qualification to avoid absolute statements where evidence does not support full certainty.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Unqualified sweeping claims undermine credibility. Appropriate qualification is a marker of academic register and epistemological awareness.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of broad empirical claims that include a qualifier
Proportion of claims so hedged they assert nothing (negative indicator)

2_min.title: Hedge It
2_min.instruction: Add a qualifier to 4 absolute claims without making them meaningless.

5_min.title: Scope Limiter
5_min.instruction: Identify 3 overgeneralised claims in a model essay. Rewrite with appropriate scope limitation.

10_min.title: Nuanced Position Statement
10_min.instruction: Write a thesis statement that presents a nuanced position (not binary agree/disagree).
10_min.success_criterion: Position includes condition or degree qualification

15_min.title: Nuance Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a binary essay introduction to introduce nuance: conditional support, contextual limitations, or degree qualifications.

failure_patterns: 'Everyone knows that...'
'This will always cause...'
Thesis with no qualifying condition where one is logically required
strength_indicators: Claims include degree qualifiers (largely, often, in many cases)
Absolute claims reserved for genuinely universal truths

micro_skills.definition: Prevent conclusions that extend beyond what the evidence or reasoning supports, particularly claims about all members of a group or universal applicability.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Overgeneralisations are a primary logical error in band 5–6 essays and indicate insufficient critical thinking regardless of linguistic quality.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of claims that make universal or unsubstantiated broad statements
Whether the scope of the generalisation matches the scope of the evidence

2_min.title: Spot the Generalisation
2_min.instruction: Identify the overgeneralisation in 4 sentences. Suggest a fix for each.

5_min.title: Evidence Scope Match
5_min.instruction: Given a claim with evidence, check if the evidence scope matches the claim scope. Adjust if needed.

10_min.title: Universals Hunt
10_min.instruction: In your last essay, circle every use of 'all', 'everyone', 'always', 'never'. Decide which need qualifying.

15_min.title: Scope-Calibrated Essay
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your introduction and thesis to eliminate all unsubstantiated universals.

failure_patterns: 'All young people spend too much time on social media'
'Technology always improves quality of life'
Conclusion that is broader than the argument supports
strength_indicators: Universal claims are absent or reserved for definitional truths
Conclusion scope matches body argument scope

micro_skills.definition: Detect the direction and pattern of change over time in data (increase, decrease, fluctuation, stability) and describe it accurately.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Trend identification is the core operation in data description tasks. Missing or mislabelling a trend is a primary task response failure.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of key trends correctly identified
Proportion of detected trends with correct direction (up/down/flat/fluctuating)

2_min.title: Trend Label Sprint
2_min.instruction: Label 6 line graph segments as: rising, falling, stable, fluctuating, peaked, bottomed.

5_min.title: Trend Sentence Set
5_min.instruction: Write 3 sentences describing 3 different trends from a given chart. Use different vocabulary for each.
5_min.success_criterion: All accurate; all use different trend vocabulary

10_min.title: Overview Trend Summary
10_min.instruction: Write a 2-sentence overview identifying the two most significant trends in a given dataset.
10_min.success_criterion: Both trends are most significant; both accurately described

15_min.title: Trend Analysis Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a full paragraph describing all trends in a line graph, with specific data references.
15_min.success_criterion: All key trends present, accurate, and supported by data

failure_patterns: Describing static values instead of changes
Confusing fluctuation with a consistent trend
Missing the overall trend while describing minor variations
strength_indicators: Trend direction always stated
Trend vocabulary varied (rose sharply, increased gradually, remained steady)

micro_skills.definition: Detect and articulate meaningful differences and similarities between data categories, groups, or time periods.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Comparison is mandatory in most data description tasks. Missing key comparisons between groups is a Task Achievement failure.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of key data comparisons mentioned in the essay
Proportion of comparisons that are factually correct

2_min.title: Comparison Extraction
2_min.instruction: From a bar chart, identify the 3 most important comparisons (largest differences).
2_min.success_criterion: All 3 are genuinely the largest differences

5_min.title: Comparison Sentence Set
5_min.instruction: Write 3 comparison sentences using while, whereas, and compared to.

10_min.title: Comparison Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph comparing two data categories across two time points.
10_min.success_criterion: Both categories and both time points addressed

15_min.title: Group Comparison Essay
15_min.instruction: Write a full Task 1 response for a bar chart with 3+ categories, prioritising group comparisons.
15_min.success_criterion: All key group comparisons present and accurate

failure_patterns: Describing each group sequentially without comparing
Comparing non-adjacent time points and missing obvious comparisons
Saying 'Group A is high; Group B is also high' without stating the relationship
strength_indicators: Comparisons use relational language (double, half, three times as many)
Most significant differences highlighted

micro_skills.definition: Detect recurring structures, regularities, or grouped behaviour across a dataset (e.g., all categories rose; developed countries consistently outperform developing ones).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Pattern recognition enables concise, high-level description rather than item-by-item listing. It is the distinguishing feature of Band 7+ data description.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of key patterns correctly identified
Whether detected patterns genuinely hold across the data

2_min.title: Pattern or Exception?
2_min.instruction: Label 5 statements about data as pattern or exception.

5_min.title: Pattern Sentence
5_min.instruction: Write one sentence that captures a pattern visible across all categories in a given chart.
5_min.success_criterion: Pattern is genuinely present in data

10_min.title: Overview Pattern Draft
10_min.instruction: Write a 2-sentence overview that describes the main pattern and one exception.

15_min.title: Pattern-Led Description
15_min.instruction: Write a Task 1 response that opens with the overall pattern before detailing specifics.
15_min.success_criterion: Pattern identified first; specifics organised under it

failure_patterns: Listing every data point without identifying patterns
Claiming a pattern based on 2/5 data points
Pattern stated but not verified across all relevant data
strength_indicators: Overview sentence generalises correctly across all categories
Exceptions to the pattern are acknowledged

micro_skills.definition: Locate and report the highest and lowest values in a dataset accurately, including the associated category and value.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Extremes are required content in virtually all data description tasks. Missing the highest or lowest value is a direct task achievement deduction.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of key extremes (max, min) mentioned
Proportion of extreme values reported accurately

2_min.title: Max/Min Drill
2_min.instruction: Identify highest and lowest values in 3 given datasets. Write one sentence for each extreme.

5_min.title: Extreme Sentence Variety
5_min.instruction: Write the same extreme fact using 3 different grammatical structures.

10_min.title: Extremes Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that highlights the highest and lowest values and places them in comparative context.
10_min.success_criterion: Both extremes present, accurate, and contextualised

15_min.title: Overview with Extremes
15_min.instruction: Write a WT1 overview that features the key extremes as supporting evidence for the main pattern.
15_min.success_criterion: Extremes accurate and integrated into pattern statement

failure_patterns: Reporting a high value but not the highest
Stating category without value or value without category
Omitting extremes in favour of describing mid-range data
strength_indicators: Highest and lowest values always named with both category and figure
Extreme values anchored in comparisons

micro_skills.definition: Accurately order data categories from highest to lowest (or vice versa) and use ordinal language to describe relative position.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Ranking language is required when comparing multiple categories. 'First', 'second', 'the most' are high-frequency WT1 requirements.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of rank statements that correctly reflect the data order
Number of distinct ordinal structures used

2_min.title: Rank 5 Items
2_min.instruction: Given 5 data values, write ranking sentences for positions 1, 3, and 5.

5_min.title: Ordinal Variety Drill
5_min.instruction: Write the same ranking using 4 different structures (the highest, ranked first, exceeded all others, had the greatest...).

10_min.title: Ranking Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph describing the ranking of 4 categories in a bar chart.
10_min.success_criterion: All 4 positions accurately described with varied language

15_min.title: Rank-Led Description
15_min.instruction: Write a full Task 1 body paragraph that organises description by rank order.
15_min.success_criterion: Rank order accurate; no repetition of ordinal structures

failure_patterns: 'Country A is high and Country B is also high' (no rank stated)
Using 'most' incorrectly when comparing two items (should be 'more')
Wrong rank order due to misreading chart
strength_indicators: Ranking language precise and varied
Relative positions stated rather than all absolute values listed

micro_skills.definition: Accurately describe fractional or percentage relationships between parts and wholes in data (e.g., pie charts, stacked bars).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Proportion vocabulary (a third, the majority, nearly half) is mandatory for pie charts and stacked data. Errors indicate inability to read this data type.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion statements match actual data values within acceptable tolerance (±5%)
Number of distinct proportion structures used

2_min.title: Proportion Match
2_min.instruction: Match 6 percentage values to their proportion expressions (52% → just over half).

5_min.title: Pie Chart Sentences
5_min.instruction: Write 3 sentences describing the proportions in a given pie chart using different vocabulary.
5_min.success_criterion: All accurate; all use different proportion language

10_min.title: Proportion Overview
10_min.instruction: Write a 3-sentence overview of a pie chart, including dominant proportion, second-largest, and combined remainder.
10_min.success_criterion: All proportions accurate and varied language used

15_min.title: Full Pie Chart Response
15_min.instruction: Write a complete Task 1 response for a pie chart, prioritising proportion language throughout.

failure_patterns: '50%' written numerically throughout without proportion vocabulary
'The majority' used for 35% (incorrect)
Confusing proportion with ranking
strength_indicators: Proportion vocabulary varied (over half, nearly a quarter, approximately two-thirds)
All proportion statements numerically accurate

micro_skills.definition: Detect and report notable exceptions, outliers, or unexpected data points that deviate from the main pattern.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Anomaly identification demonstrates analytical precision. It distinguishes mechanical description from genuine data reading.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of notable anomalies mentioned in response
Anomaly correctly framed as exception to stated pattern

confidence_formula.formula: confidence = 0.7 if anomaly is detectable else 0.3

2_min.title: Exception Spotter
2_min.instruction: Given a dataset with one anomaly, identify it and write a sentence flagging it.
2_min.success_criterion: Correct anomaly identified and framed as exception

5_min.title: Pattern + Exception Sentence Pair
5_min.instruction: Write the main pattern sentence, then a contrast sentence for the exception.

10_min.title: Exception Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that establishes the pattern, then introduces and contextualises one anomaly.
10_min.success_criterion: Anomaly framed as exception to stated pattern

15_min.title: Anomaly-Enriched Response
15_min.instruction: Write a full Task 1 response that includes at least one exception framed within the overall pattern.

failure_patterns: Anomaly included but not identified as exception — treated as part of the pattern
Notable outlier missed entirely
Spending more words on anomaly than on main pattern
strength_indicators: Exception framed with contrast language (however, unlike, with the exception of)
Anomaly proportion of response is smaller than main pattern coverage

micro_skills.definition: Condense the essential information from a dataset or text into a concise, accurate, and prioritised summary without over-detailing or omitting key points.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: The WT1 overview is a direct test of summarisation. Poor summarisation produces either copying (too detailed) or vagueness (too general).

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of key information points included in summary
Summary length as proportion of full response (target: 15–20%)
Proportion of summary that is verbatim copied from prompt (lower is better)

2_min.title: Two-Sentence Summary
2_min.instruction: Summarise a given paragraph in exactly 2 sentences. No copying.
2_min.success_criterion: Both key points present; no verbatim phrases

5_min.title: Overview Draft
5_min.instruction: Write a 2-sentence WT1 overview for a given chart. Identify main feature and most significant difference.
5_min.success_criterion: Both elements present; no data copied directly

10_min.title: Rank and Summarise
10_min.instruction: List 5 data points in priority order (most to least important to report). Write a summary using the top 3.
10_min.success_criterion: Priority order defensible; summary focuses on top 3

15_min.title: Overview + Body Summary
15_min.instruction: Write a WT1 overview and a body paragraph that summarises the most important data group.
15_min.success_criterion: Overview is general; body adds selective detail

failure_patterns: Copying data table values without synthesis
Overview contains no general statement — only specific figures
Summary omits the most important trend
strength_indicators: Overview uses no specific data — only general patterns
Body paragraphs prioritise key data and avoid exhaustive listing

micro_skills.definition: Choose which data points or content elements to include in a response based on their significance, rather than attempting to report everything.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Exhaustive listing of data without selection is a Band 5 pattern. Selection demonstrates analytical prioritisation.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of included data points that are among the most significant
Proportion of included data points that are minor or redundant (lower is better)

2_min.title: Top 3 Selection
2_min.instruction: From a list of 8 data points, select the 3 most important to report. Justify.
2_min.success_criterion: 3 selected are among the most significant

5_min.title: Cut the Minor Data
5_min.instruction: Given a WT1 draft that over-reports, identify 3 data points to remove. Justify.
5_min.success_criterion: Removed data points are genuinely minor

10_min.title: Data Priority Plan
10_min.instruction: Before writing, rank all data points by significance. Plan which paragraph each will appear in.
10_min.success_criterion: Top-ranked data in overview; medium in body; minor excluded

15_min.title: Selective Task 1 Response
15_min.instruction: Write a WT1 response selecting only the most significant data. Do not include every figure.
15_min.success_criterion: No minor data included; all major features covered

failure_patterns: Reporting every data point in the chart
Including minor fluctuations while missing the main trend
Overview that contains too many specific figures
strength_indicators: Fewer data points used; each one significant
Body organised by feature significance, not data order

micro_skills.definition: Write an opening paragraph that contextualises the topic, signals the essay's direction, and meets genre-specific requirements (e.g., paraphrased prompt + thesis for WT2; overview for WT1).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A weak introduction fails to establish position and structure, causing both TR and CC penalties from the first paragraph.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of required introduction components present
Introduction paraphrases prompt without copying; 0=copied, 0.5=partial, 1.0=full paraphrase

2_min.title: Paraphrase Drill
2_min.instruction: Paraphrase a given prompt sentence in 2 different ways. No word copying.
2_min.success_criterion: Both convey same meaning; no key words repeated

5_min.title: Introduction Anatomy
5_min.instruction: Label each sentence of 3 model introductions: background / position / scope / overview.

10_min.title: Introduction Draft
10_min.instruction: Write a complete introduction for a given prompt. Include all required components.
10_min.success_criterion: All required components present; no verbatim copying

15_min.title: Three Introduction Comparison
15_min.instruction: Write 3 introductions for the same prompt using different paraphrase strategies. Evaluate which is strongest.
15_min.success_criterion: All 3 valid; evaluation shows genuine discrimination

failure_patterns: Copying prompt verbatim
Missing thesis in WT2 introduction
Introduction that begins 'In today's world...' without engaging the prompt
strength_indicators: Introduction paraphrase uses synonyms and restructured syntax
Thesis clearly signals the essay's position and structure

micro_skills.definition: Formulate a clear, specific, arguable thesis statement that directly responds to the prompt and forecasts the essay's main argument(s).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: The thesis is the essay's navigation instrument. A vague or absent thesis causes both task response failure and structural incoherence.

evaluation_metrics.description: A thesis statement is identifiable in the introduction
Thesis names a specific position, not a vague statement of intent
Essay body arguments match the thesis claim(s)

2_min.title: Thesis or Not?
2_min.instruction: Label 5 sentences as thesis or non-thesis. Justify.

5_min.title: Weak → Strong Thesis
5_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 vague thesis statements to specific, arguable ones.

10_min.title: Thesis-to-Body Alignment
10_min.instruction: Check whether your last essay's body paragraphs deliver what the thesis promised. List mismatches.

15_min.title: Thesis-First Writing
15_min.instruction: Write a thesis. Then write 3 topic sentences that fulfil the thesis. Verify alignment.
15_min.success_criterion: All topic sentences directly advance the thesis

failure_patterns: 'This essay will discuss both sides' (meta-commentary, not a thesis)
Thesis that lists topics instead of taking a position
Thesis not matched by body arguments
strength_indicators: Thesis makes a clear claim that could be argued against
Body paragraph claims are derivations of thesis

micro_skills.definition: Allocate content to paragraphs before writing, ensuring each paragraph has a single clear focus and that the essay as a whole covers all required components.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Unplanned essays produce overloaded multi-topic paragraphs and missing components. Pre-writing paragraph plans are associated with higher band scores.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of paragraphs with a single identifiable topic
All required essay components addressed across paragraphs

2_min.title: Paragraph Allocation
2_min.instruction: Assign 6 given ideas to the correct paragraphs in a 4-paragraph essay plan.

5_min.title: 5-Minute Plan
5_min.instruction: Write a full paragraph plan for a given prompt in 5 minutes.
5_min.success_criterion: All required components assigned to a paragraph

10_min.title: Plan Critique
10_min.instruction: Evaluate a given essay plan. Identify multi-topic paragraphs and missing components.

15_min.title: Plan Then Write
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph plan. Then write 2 body paragraphs that follow the plan exactly.
15_min.success_criterion: Paragraphs match plan; each has single focus

failure_patterns: Paragraph 2 contains 3 different ideas
Two paragraphs that repeat the same content
No plan leads to missing conclusion
strength_indicators: Each paragraph has exactly one main idea traceable to a topic sentence
Essay covers all required components without overlap

micro_skills.definition: Open each body paragraph with a sentence that clearly states the paragraph's main idea, directly links to the thesis, and signals what follows.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Topic sentences are the primary structural signal in English academic writing. Their absence makes paragraphs appear as undifferentiated content blocks.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of body paragraphs with an identifiable topic sentence as first or second sentence
Topic sentences advance or support the thesis

2_min.title: Topic Sentence Sort
2_min.instruction: Identify the topic sentence in 4 given paragraphs.

5_min.title: Topic Sentence Writing
5_min.instruction: Write topic sentences for 3 paragraph ideas, each linked to a given thesis.
5_min.success_criterion: All 3 claim something and link to thesis

10_min.title: Topic Sentence Alignment Check
10_min.instruction: Check your last essay: do all topic sentences advance the thesis? List any that don't.

15_min.title: Paragraph Rebuild
15_min.instruction: Given 3 scrambled paragraphs, write topic sentences for each and place them correctly.
15_min.success_criterion: Each topic sentence accurately forecasts paragraph content

failure_patterns: Paragraph starts with an example instead of a claim
Topic sentence is a transition from previous paragraph, not a new claim
Topic sentence covers two different ideas
strength_indicators: Topic sentences make arguable claims
First word of each body paragraph is not a discourse marker

micro_skills.definition: Distribute word count and development effort proportionally across paragraphs according to their relative importance to the task.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Imbalanced essays (e.g., 200 words in paragraph 1, 50 in paragraph 2) create structural incoherence and suggest planning failure.

evaluation_metrics.description: Standard deviation of body paragraph lengths (lower = more balanced)
Proportion of paragraphs with < 70% of average body paragraph length

2_min.title: Balance Audit
2_min.instruction: Count words in each paragraph of your last essay. Is any paragraph less than 60% of the longest?

5_min.title: Proportion Planning
5_min.instruction: Plan word allocation per paragraph for a 250-word essay. Target ≤ 20% variance between body paragraphs.

10_min.title: Expand the Thin Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Identify your shortest body paragraph. Add 2 sentences without padding.
10_min.success_criterion: New sentences add genuine content; balance improved

15_min.title: Balanced Essay Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a 4-paragraph essay targeting equal body paragraph lengths (±15%).
15_min.success_criterion: Body paragraphs within ±15% of each other

failure_patterns: First body paragraph 3x length of second
Conclusion longer than any body paragraph
One paragraph is a single sentence
strength_indicators: Body paragraphs within 20% of each other in word count
All paragraphs equally developed

micro_skills.definition: Write a closing paragraph that synthesises the essay's main arguments, restates the position, and provides closure without introducing new content.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A conclusion that introduces new ideas, merely repeats the introduction, or is absent produces both TR and CC penalties.

evaluation_metrics.description: A conclusion paragraph is present
No new main arguments introduced in conclusion
Conclusion restates the thesis position (not copied)

2_min.title: Conclusion Anatomy
2_min.instruction: Label each sentence of 3 model conclusions: restatement / synthesis / position / closing.

5_min.title: Position Restatement
5_min.instruction: Rewrite a thesis as a conclusion restatement without copying any words.

10_min.title: Conclusion Draft
10_min.instruction: Write a conclusion for a given essay: restate position + synthesise main arguments in 2–3 sentences.
10_min.success_criterion: Position restated; main arguments synthesised; no new content

15_min.title: Intro-Conclusion Mirror Check
15_min.instruction: Write matching introduction and conclusion for a given essay. Both must cover same content in different language.
15_min.success_criterion: Same position in both; <30% shared vocabulary

failure_patterns: Conclusion introduces a new argument
Conclusion copies introduction verbatim
No conclusion — essay ends with last body paragraph
strength_indicators: Conclusion opens with a synthesis signal (In conclusion, Overall, To summarise)
Position clearly restated in different language from introduction

micro_skills.definition: Organise the entire essay so that the sequence of paragraphs is logical, each section builds on the previous, and the essay reads as a unified whole.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Individual good paragraphs do not produce a good essay if their order is arbitrary. Global structure is a Band 7+ differentiator for CC.

evaluation_metrics.description: Each paragraph follows logically from the previous (no abrupt topic shifts)
Essay has: introduction → body → conclusion in correct order

2_min.title: Paragraph Sequencing
2_min.instruction: Reorder 4 scrambled paragraphs into the most logical sequence.

5_min.title: Logic Gap Detection
5_min.instruction: In a given essay, identify the two paragraph transitions that feel most abrupt.

10_min.title: Structural Outline Critique
10_min.instruction: Review an essay outline. Identify any structural logic problems. Propose fix.

15_min.title: Structured Essay Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a full essay where each paragraph's topic sentence explicitly links forward from the previous paragraph.

failure_patterns: Counterargument placed before main arguments
Causes discussed after solutions
Conclusion appears in the middle of the essay
strength_indicators: Paragraphs follow a clear logical sequence (general to specific, problem to solution, position to counter to rebuttal)
No paragraph could be removed without breaking the argumentative chain

micro_skills.definition: Order information within a paragraph so that each sentence follows naturally from the previous, building meaning progressively.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Illogical within-paragraph sequencing produces CC penalties even when each sentence is individually correct.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of adjacent sentence pairs where the second follows logically from the first
Information is ordered from general to specific or follows a logical development pattern

2_min.title: Sentence Reorder
2_min.instruction: Reorder 5 scrambled sentences into a coherent paragraph.

5_min.title: Sequence Logic Check
5_min.instruction: In your last body paragraph, check each sentence pair. Mark any that feel out of order.

10_min.title: Paragraph Rebuild
10_min.instruction: Take a paragraph with sequencing problems. Rewrite with correct order.

15_min.title: Sentence-by-Sentence Planning
15_min.instruction: Before writing a paragraph, write a 6-sentence plan where each sentence is labelled by its function.

failure_patterns: Example appears before the claim it illustrates
Explanation placed before the claim it explains
Conclusion sentence placed in the middle of the paragraph
strength_indicators: Claim → explanation → evidence → link sequence is consistent
No sentence is isolated from its neighbours

micro_skills.definition: Use discourse markers and transition phrases accurately and appropriately to signal logical relationships between sentences and paragraphs.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Overuse, underuse, or misuse of transitions are among the most commonly penalised CC errors. 'Furthermore' used to introduce a contradictory point is a Band 5 marker.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of transitions that correctly signal their stated logical relationship
Proportion of sentences beginning with a discourse marker (target: < 40%)
Number of distinct transition types used

score_formula.formula: score = transition_accuracy_ratio * (1 - max(0, transition_overuse_penalty - 0.4)) * min(1.0, transition_variety / 5)

2_min.title: Transition Match
2_min.instruction: Match 6 transitions to their correct logical function (addition, contrast, cause, result, example, concession).

5_min.title: Misuse Detection
5_min.instruction: Identify 3 incorrectly used transitions in a given paragraph. Replace each with the correct choice.

10_min.title: Transition Variety Rewrite
10_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph that uses 'Furthermore / Moreover / Additionally' for every sentence. Use structurally varied cohesion instead.
10_min.success_criterion: Zero repeated transitions; all logical relationships maintained

15_min.title: Cohesion Audit
15_min.instruction: Audit your last essay's transitions. Count types used, identify overused ones, replace 3.

failure_patterns: 'Furthermore' used for a contrasting point
Every sentence begins with 'However, Moreover, Furthermore, Therefore'
No transitions between paragraphs
strength_indicators: Transition type matches logical relationship
Mix of sentence-initial markers and embedded cohesive devices

micro_skills.definition: Use pronouns, demonstratives, and nominal substitutions accurately so that all referents are unambiguous.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Ambiguous reference is a primary CC error at all levels. 'It' with no clear antecedent forces the reader to backtrack.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of ambiguous pronoun or demonstrative references in essay
Proportion of reference items with unambiguous antecedents

2_min.title: Reference Trace
2_min.instruction: For each pronoun in 4 sentences, draw an arrow to its antecedent. Mark any ambiguous.

5_min.title: Ambiguity Repair
5_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 sentences with ambiguous pronouns to make the referent clear.

10_min.title: Reference Audit
10_min.instruction: In your last essay, circle every 'it', 'this', 'they', 'these'. Verify each has a clear antecedent.

15_min.title: Clear Reference Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph with ≥ 3 ambiguous references. Make every referent unambiguous.

failure_patterns: 'This is important' — 'this' could refer to any of three things in previous sentence
'They' used to refer to a singular noun
Pronoun used before its antecedent is introduced
strength_indicators: Every pronoun has an unambiguous antecedent within 2 sentences
Demonstratives ('this argument', 'these factors') use noun phrases, not bare 'this'

micro_skills.definition: Structure sentences so that given (known) information precedes new information, creating a smooth reading experience without cognitive backtracking.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Poor information flow is the underlying cause of 'choppy' writing that reads as disjointed even when transitions are present.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of sentence pairs where new sentence opens with given information from previous
Degree of consistent topic development across consecutive sentences

2_min.title: Given-New Sentence Pair
2_min.instruction: Connect 3 sentence pairs so that sentence 2 opens with information from sentence 1.

5_min.title: Flow Repair
5_min.instruction: Rewrite a choppy 4-sentence sequence by reordering information within sentences.

10_min.title: Chain Development
10_min.instruction: Write a 5-sentence paragraph where each sentence explicitly picks up a concept from the previous.
10_min.success_criterion: Each sentence contains a reference to the previous sentence's key term

15_min.title: Flow Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last body paragraph to optimise given-new information structure.

failure_patterns: Each sentence introduces a new concept without linking to the previous
Paragraph reads like a list of separate ideas
Abrupt introduction of new entities mid-paragraph
strength_indicators: Sentences chain naturally; reading requires no backtracking
Paragraph develops a single thread progressively

micro_skills.definition: Embed examples cohesively within the paragraph flow using appropriate framing structures rather than dropping them as isolated statements.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Poorly integrated examples disrupt paragraph flow and signal low CC. This is a different skill from generating examples — it is about embedding them smoothly.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of examples introduced with a framing structure (for example, such as, for instance, this is illustrated by)
Examples that appear to interrupt the paragraph logic rather than support it

2_min.title: Frame 3 Examples
2_min.instruction: Insert appropriate framing phrases before 3 bare example sentences.

5_min.title: Disruption Detection
5_min.instruction: Identify the example in a given paragraph that disrupts flow. Explain why. Rewrite.
5_min.success_criterion: Disruption correctly identified; rewrite improves flow

10_min.title: Integrated Example Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph where one example is introduced, framed, presented, and linked back to claim.

15_min.title: Multi-Example Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph with 2 examples, each smoothly integrated. Vary the framing structures.
15_min.success_criterion: Both examples framed and followed by links; no flow disruption

failure_patterns: Example dropped mid-paragraph without introduction
'For example' used twice in the same paragraph
Example never returned to — it floats without a link
strength_indicators: Every example has a framing phrase and a follow-up link
Examples advance rather than interrupt paragraph logic

micro_skills.definition: Ensure that each paragraph and sentence moves the argument, description, or analysis forward rather than cycling back to previously stated content.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Circular writing — restating earlier points in new words — is a primary CC and LR penalty marker. Progression is essential for Band 7+.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of sentences that add new information not already stated
Number of sentences that restate a previously made point in different words

2_min.title: Forward or Repeat?
2_min.instruction: Label 6 sentences as forward (adds new content) or repeat (restates previous).

5_min.title: Repetition Hunt
5_min.instruction: Find and cut 2 repetitive sentences from a given paragraph. Verify argument is still complete.
5_min.success_criterion: Both are genuine repeats; argument intact after removal

10_min.title: Progress Map
10_min.instruction: For each sentence in your last body paragraph, write 3 words summarising its unique contribution.
10_min.success_criterion: Each sentence has a distinct contribution

15_min.title: Zero Repetition Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph to eliminate all repetition. Each sentence must add new content.
15_min.success_criterion: Zero repetitive sentences; argument still complete

failure_patterns: Conclusion paragraph that repeats introduction with no synthesis
Three sentences that all say 'technology has many benefits'
Padding at the end of a paragraph to reach word count
strength_indicators: Every sentence in a paragraph is necessary — removing any would create a gap
Conclusion synthesises rather than restates

micro_skills.definition: Achieve paragraph and essay cohesion through a range of cohesive mechanisms (substitution, ellipsis, lexical chains, reference) rather than relying exclusively on explicit discourse markers.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Mechanical overuse of discourse markers at every sentence boundary is an explicit Band 5 descriptor in IELTS CC. It also affects readability in all genres.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of distinct cohesive mechanisms used (discourse markers, pronoun reference, lexical chain, substitution, ellipsis)
Proportion of sentences starting with a discourse marker (target < 0.35)

score_formula.formula: score = min(1.0, cohesive_device_variety_score / 4) * max(0, 1 - max(0, discourse_marker_sentence_start_ratio - 0.35) * 3)

2_min.title: Cohesion Type Inventory
2_min.instruction: In a given paragraph, identify one example of each cohesive device: marker, pronoun reference, lexical chain, substitution.

5_min.title: Remove the Markers
5_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph so that no sentence starts with a discourse marker. Maintain coherence.

10_min.title: Lexical Chain Build
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that creates cohesion through lexical chains (synonyms, hypernyms, related words) rather than markers.
10_min.success_criterion: Evaluator identifies lexical chain; ≤ 1 discourse marker used

15_min.title: Cohesion Variety Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite an overlinking paragraph using a minimum of 4 different cohesive mechanisms.

failure_patterns: Firstly, Secondly, Thirdly, Furthermore, Moreover, Therefore — every sentence
No cohesion devices at all (completely disconnected)
Same 2 transitions used throughout entire essay
strength_indicators: Mix of implicit and explicit cohesion
Lexical chains carry cohesion across paragraph without markers

micro_skills.definition: Select the word whose denotative and connotative meaning is the most accurate fit for the intended meaning in context.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Imprecise word choice distorts meaning and signals limited LR control. Precision is the primary LR quality marker at Band 7+.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of words whose meaning is close but not exact for the context
Proportion of content words rated as precise for context

2_min.title: Close But Not Right
2_min.instruction: For 4 highlighted words, identify a more precise replacement.

5_min.title: Precision Spectrum
5_min.instruction: For a given concept, list 5 words from vague to precise. Use the most precise in a sentence.

10_min.title: Precision Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. Highlight 5 words and identify a more precise replacement for each.

15_min.title: Precision Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph replacing all imprecise content words with precise alternatives.

failure_patterns: 'affect' used when 'harm', 'impair', or 'damage' is more precise
'big' used where 'significant', 'substantial', or 'considerable' is required
Vague nominals ('thing', 'issue', 'aspect') throughout
strength_indicators: Content words are specific and contextually accurate
