No use of vague all-purpose nouns

micro_skills.definition: Produce word combinations that are conventionally used by proficient speakers (e.g., 'raise awareness', 'make a decision', 'heavy traffic') rather than logically constructed but unnatural pairings.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Collocation errors are the most common LR error at B2 and are penalised as 'errors in word choice' in IELTS. They often pass grammar checks but signal non-native production.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of collocation errors detected
Proportion of tested word pairs that are conventional collocations

2_min.title: Natural or Not?
2_min.instruction: Label 6 word pairs as natural collocations or unusual.

5_min.title: Error Correction
5_min.instruction: Correct 4 collocation errors in a paragraph.

10_min.title: Collocation Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. List 5 word pairs you used. Verify each is a natural collocation.

15_min.title: Collocation-Enriched Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph replacing unnatural word pairs with correct collocations.

failure_patterns: 'do a decision' (make a decision)
'strong rain' (heavy rain)
'rise awareness' (raise awareness)
'economical development' (economic development)
strength_indicators: Word pairs are conventional and natural-sounding
Academic collocations used accurately (conduct research, draw conclusions, take into account)

micro_skills.definition: Ensure that all words in a phrase or sentence are semantically compatible — that their meanings do not contradict or create unintended logical relationships.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Semantic errors (e.g., 'the government should abolish taxes to increase government revenue') produce sentences that are grammatically correct but meaninglessly contradictory.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of semantic incompatibility errors detected
Proportion of sentences with no semantic conflicts

2_min.title: Semantic Clash Detector
2_min.instruction: Identify the semantic error in 4 given sentences.

5_min.title: Compatibility Repair
5_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 semantically incoherent sentences.

10_min.title: Semantic Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay sentence by sentence. Flag any semantic contradictions.

15_min.title: Semantic Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph with 3 semantic errors. Verify semantic coherence after.

failure_patterns: 'The government should reduce spending to improve public services'
'Banning smoking will cause smokers to become healthier' (circular)
Using antonyms when synonyms are intended
strength_indicators: All word-level relationships within sentences are logically consistent
No self-contradicting propositions

micro_skills.definition: Consistently select vocabulary appropriate to the formality level of the writing context, avoiding informal words in formal contexts and vice versa.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Register violation is one of the most common LR penalties. A single informal word in an academic essay signals insufficient awareness of genre constraints.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of informal words/phrases detected in formal context
Proportion of content words appropriate to target register

2_min.title: Formal or Informal?
2_min.instruction: Label 8 words/phrases as formal or informal.

5_min.title: Formalise a Paragraph
5_min.instruction: Replace 4 informal words in a paragraph with formal equivalents.
5_min.success_criterion: All 4 formal and correct in context

10_min.title: Register Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. Flag any informal words, contractions, or colloquial phrases.

15_min.title: Register Consistent Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a mixed-register paragraph so that every word is appropriate to academic register.

failure_patterns: 'a lot of' in academic essay (use 'a significant number of', 'considerable', 'substantial')
Contractions in formal writing
'kids' instead of 'children'
'nowadays' used repeatedly
strength_indicators: Vocabulary consistently formal throughout
No contractions; no colloquialisms; no conversational filler

micro_skills.definition: Restate source text or prompt language in own words using synonyms, structural changes, and perspective shifts without changing meaning.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Paraphrasing is tested directly in IELTS introduction. Copying prompt language is penalised. Paraphrasing quality also indicates LR range.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of content words from source that are changed in paraphrase
Paraphrase preserves original meaning without distortion

2_min.title: Quick Paraphrase
2_min.instruction: Paraphrase a given sentence without using any of its key words.
2_min.success_criterion: Same meaning; no key words repeated

5_min.title: Three-Way Paraphrase
5_min.instruction: Paraphrase one sentence in 3 different ways using synonym substitution, structural change, and perspective shift.
5_min.success_criterion: All 3 use different strategies; all preserve meaning

10_min.title: Introduction Paraphrase
10_min.instruction: Write an introduction that paraphrases the full prompt. Change ≥ 70% of content words.

15_min.title: Paraphrase Comparison
15_min.instruction: Write 3 versions of a prompt paraphrase at different quality levels. Evaluate each.
15_min.success_criterion: Quality levels clearly distinguishable in evaluation

failure_patterns: Copying prompt sentence with 1–2 word changes
Paraphrase changes meaning (over-substitution)
Structural paraphrase only — same words, different order
strength_indicators: Lexical change ratio ≥ 0.7
Structural transformation plus synonym substitution used together

micro_skills.definition: Use a range of vocabulary rather than repeating the same high-frequency words, demonstrating breadth of lexical resource.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Lexical variety is explicitly assessed in IELTS LR. Repetition of limited vocabulary is penalised under 'narrow range'.

evaluation_metrics.description: Ratio of unique words to total words (normalised for essay length)
Content words used > 3 times in the same essay

2_min.title: Synonym Sprint
2_min.instruction: Write 4 synonyms for each of: 'important', 'increase', 'cause', 'affect'.

5_min.title: Repetition Audit
5_min.instruction: List all content words used 3+ times in your last essay. Replace each with a varied alternative.

10_min.title: Lexical Expansion Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph to replace all repeated content words. No content word should appear twice.
10_min.success_criterion: Type-token ratio improved; all replacements accurate

15_min.title: Variety-Focused Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph consciously avoiding repetition. After writing, check every content word for repeats.
15_min.success_criterion: No content word repeated; all synonyms accurate

failure_patterns: 'Problem' used 8 times in a 250-word essay
'Important' or 'significant' repeated throughout
Same example vocabulary recycled across paragraphs
strength_indicators: Key concepts expressed with varied vocabulary throughout essay
Type-token ratio ≥ 0.5 for academic essays

micro_skills.definition: Deploy topic-specific and academic vocabulary accurately in context, including correct part-of-speech usage and contextual appropriateness.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Topic-specific vocabulary is required for precision and is a positive LR indicator. Misuse of specialist terms is penalised.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of topic-specific vocabulary items used correctly in context
Number of topic-specific vocabulary items per 100 words

2_min.title: Topic Vocabulary Build
2_min.instruction: List 6 topic-specific words for a given subject area. Write each in a sentence.

5_min.title: Misuse Detection
5_min.instruction: Identify 3 misused topic vocabulary items in a given paragraph. Correct each.

10_min.title: Vocabulary-in-Context
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph on a given topic using 5 pre-specified topic vocabulary items correctly.
10_min.success_criterion: All 5 items used correctly in context

15_min.title: Topic Vocabulary Plan
15_min.instruction: For a given essay topic, build a vocabulary map: 10 key terms, their parts of speech, and example collocations.

failure_patterns: Using 'economical' when 'economic' is required
Applying technical vocabulary outside its domain
Topic vocabulary present but in wrong part-of-speech form
strength_indicators: Topic vocabulary appears in accurate collocations
No misuse of specialist terms

micro_skills.definition: Accurately form nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs through correct use of prefixes, suffixes, and derivational morphology.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Word form errors (e.g., 'successfully' as a verb, 'education' where 'educational' is needed) are among the most frequent LR errors and are straightforwardly penalised.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of incorrect word form choices
Proportion of derivational choices that are correct

2_min.title: Form Transformation
2_min.instruction: Convert 6 given base words into all 4 forms: noun, verb, adjective, adverb.

5_min.title: Error Correction
5_min.instruction: Identify and correct 5 word form errors in a given paragraph.

10_min.title: Word Form Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for word form errors. List each with the correct form.

15_min.title: Derivation-Conscious Writing
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph using 5 word families, deliberately varying the form in each instance.

failure_patterns: 'The economy improve' (noun form used as verb)
'Educate children is important' (verb form as noun without gerund)
'-tion' suffix attached to an adjective
strength_indicators: All word forms correct in context
Word family variation used deliberately for lexical variety

micro_skills.definition: Produce correctly spelled words, including irregular spellings, academic vocabulary, and topic-specific terms.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Spelling errors are mechanically penalised in LR. Consistent spelling errors in academic vocabulary indicate low written language exposure.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of spelling errors in essay
Proportion of words spelled correctly

2_min.title: Spelling Correction Drill
2_min.instruction: Find and correct 8 spelling errors in a given paragraph.

5_min.title: Academic Spelling List
5_min.instruction: Write the correct spelling of 10 commonly misspelled academic words from memory.

10_min.title: Spelling Audit
10_min.instruction: Read your last essay. Circle every word you are uncertain about. Look up each.

15_min.title: Targeted Spelling Practice
15_min.instruction: Take your 10 most-misspelled words. Write each in 3 different sentences.
15_min.success_criterion: Target words spelled correctly in all instances

failure_patterns: 'accomodate' (accommodate)
'enviroment' (environment)
'goverment' (government)
Systematic errors in a category (e.g., -tion vs. -sion)
strength_indicators: Zero spelling errors in academic vocabulary
Consistent correct spelling of topic-specific terms

micro_skills.definition: Correctly select a/an, the, or zero article based on countability, specificity, generic reference, and contextual definiteness.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Article errors are among the highest-frequency GRA errors for speakers whose L1 has no article system. They are consistently penalised.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of article errors
Proportion of article slots with correct choice

2_min.title: Article Fill
2_min.instruction: Fill 10 article gaps in sentences. Justify each choice.

5_min.title: Error Correction
5_min.instruction: Correct 5 article errors in a paragraph.

10_min.title: Article Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for article errors. List rule violated for each error.

15_min.title: Article-Aware Writing
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph where you justify every article choice in a comment next to each sentence.

failure_patterns: 'The government should invest in education' (correct) vs. 'A government should invest in the education' (incorrect)
Zero article with singular countable nouns
'The' with uncountable or plural generic nouns
strength_indicators: No article errors in a 250-word essay
Correct use of 'the' for second mention and specific reference

micro_skills.definition: Correctly form singular and plural nouns, including irregular plurals, uncountable nouns, and nominalisations.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Noun form errors (incorrect pluralisation, countability violations) are penalised under GRA accuracy.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of noun form errors
Proportion of noun forms correctly produced

2_min.title: Plural Forms
2_min.instruction: Write the plural of 10 given nouns including 3 irregular.

5_min.title: Countability Sort
5_min.instruction: Sort 8 nouns into countable, uncountable, or both. Write one sentence using each correctly.
5_min.success_criterion: All nouns correctly sorted; all sentences grammatical

10_min.title: Noun Form Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for noun form errors.

15_min.title: Nominalisation Practice
15_min.instruction: Rewrite 5 verbal sentences using nominalisation. Check all noun forms.

failure_patterns: 'informations' (uncountable)
'advices' (uncountable)
'criterias' (criteria is already plural)
Treating mass nouns as countable
strength_indicators: All uncountable nouns treated correctly
Irregular plurals produced accurately

micro_skills.definition: Ensure subject-verb and noun-pronoun agreement across all sentence types including complex structures with intervening phrases.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Agreement errors are highly visible to readers and are penalised under GRA accuracy. They persist in complex sentences even when simple sentences are correct.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of subject-verb and noun-pronoun agreement errors
Proportion of agreement targets that are correct

2_min.title: Agreement Error Hunt
2_min.instruction: Find and correct 5 agreement errors in a given paragraph.

5_min.title: Complex Subject Agreement
5_min.instruction: Write 5 sentences where the subject is a complex noun phrase. Ensure all verbs agree.

10_min.title: Agreement Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for agreement errors.

15_min.title: Complex Sentence Agreement
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph with 3 complex sentences. After writing, verify every agreement target.

failure_patterns: 'The number of people are...' (singular subject)
'Each of the students have...' (singular agreement)
Agreement lost after long intervening phrase
strength_indicators: Agreement maintained in sentences with relative clauses and intervening phrases
No agreement errors across 250 words

micro_skills.definition: Select and consistently maintain appropriate verb tenses for the context, including narrative tense sequences and academic present conventions.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Tense errors are penalised as GRA accuracy failures. Inconsistent tense shifts create CC problems and signal lack of grammatical control.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of tense selection or consistency errors
Proportion of verbs in contextually appropriate tense

2_min.title: Tense Selection Drill
2_min.instruction: Select the correct tense for 8 given verbs in context.

5_min.title: Tense Consistency Check
5_min.instruction: Find all tense shifts in a given paragraph. Decide which are errors and which are intentional.

10_min.title: Tense Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for tense errors.

15_min.title: Tense-Controlled Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that mixes past data reference with present analysis. Mark tense choice for each verb.
15_min.success_criterion: All tense choices defensible; zero errors

failure_patterns: Switching from present to past mid-paragraph with no logical reason
'The data showed that prices rises' (inconsistent tense)
Using future tense throughout an academic analysis
strength_indicators: Tense consistent with genre conventions
Past tense for historical data, present for analysis — consistently applied

micro_skills.definition: Select the correct preposition in fixed and semi-fixed prepositional phrases, including verb + preposition and adjective + preposition constructions.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Preposition errors are among the most frequent GRA accuracy failures. Many preposition choices are arbitrary (depend on vs. depend in) and must be memorised.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of preposition errors
Proportion of preposition slots with correct choice

2_min.title: Preposition Fill
2_min.instruction: Fill 8 preposition gaps in given phrases.

5_min.title: Error Correction
5_min.instruction: Correct 5 preposition errors in a paragraph.

10_min.title: Preposition Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for preposition errors.

15_min.title: Preposition-Focused Writing
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph using 8 target verb/adjective + preposition phrases. Check each.

failure_patterns: 'depend in' (depend on)
'responsible of' (responsible for)
'interested on' (interested in)
'consist in' (consist of)
strength_indicators: All fixed prepositional phrases correct
No preposition errors in verb/adjective collocations

micro_skills.definition: Use commas, semicolons, colons, and full stops correctly according to syntactic function.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Punctuation errors (comma splices, missing commas after adverbials, incorrect semicolons) affect readability and are penalised under GRA accuracy.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of punctuation errors
Proportion of punctuation slots correctly used

2_min.title: Punctuation Error Hunt
2_min.instruction: Find and correct 6 punctuation errors in a paragraph.

5_min.title: Comma Rule Application
5_min.instruction: Add correct punctuation to 5 unpunctuated complex sentences.

10_min.title: Punctuation Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for punctuation errors. List the rule violated for each.

15_min.title: Punctuation-Focused Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph using: 1 colon, 1 semicolon, 3 subordinate-clause commas, and 1 list comma. All correct.

failure_patterns: Comma splice: 'I went home, I was tired'
No comma after fronted adverbial
Semicolon used as colon
Missing commas in relative clauses
strength_indicators: No comma splices
Consistent correct punctuation in complex sentences

micro_skills.definition: Produce grammatically complete simple sentences with correct subject-verb-object order and necessary modifiers.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Foundation of all sentence construction. Simple sentence errors indicate fundamental GRA issues that compound in complex structures.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of errors in simple sentences
Proportion of simple sentences that are grammatically complete and correct

2_min.title: Fragment Repair
2_min.instruction: Identify and fix 4 sentence fragments.

5_min.title: Clean Simple Sentences
5_min.instruction: Write 5 complete simple sentences on a given topic. No grammar errors.

10_min.title: Sentence Audit
10_min.instruction: Isolate all simple sentences in your last essay. Check each for completeness.

15_min.title: Error-Free Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph using only simple sentences. Target zero errors.

failure_patterns: Sentence fragments
Run-on sentences without punctuation
Missing main verb
strength_indicators: All simple sentences grammatically complete
Clear SVO structure maintained

micro_skills.definition: Produce grammatically correct sentences with at least one dependent clause, using appropriate subordinators, punctuation, and clause ordering.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Complex sentence use is a primary GRA range indicator. Absence of complex sentences caps GRA at Band 5. Errors in complex sentences (wrong subordinator, incorrect punctuation) cap at Band 6.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of sentences containing at least one dependent clause
Proportion of complex sentences that are error-free
Proportion of attempted complex sentences that are both attempted and error-free

2_min.title: Clause Adding
2_min.instruction: Add a dependent clause to 4 simple sentences. Vary the subordinator.
2_min.success_criterion: All grammatically correct; all different subordinators

5_min.title: Subordinator Match
5_min.instruction: Match 6 clause pairs to the correct subordinator (although, because, if, when, while, unless).

10_min.title: Complex Sentence Audit
10_min.instruction: In your last essay, identify all complex sentences. Check each for errors.
10_min.success_criterion: All errors found; complex sentence ratio calculated

15_min.title: Complex Sentence Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph where ≥ 60% of sentences are complex. Target zero errors.

failure_patterns: 'Although X, but Y' (double subordination)
Dependent clause as a sentence fragment
Wrong subordinator for logical relationship (while used for cause, not contrast)
strength_indicators: Wide range of subordinators used accurately
Complex sentences used to express logical relationships (cause, contrast, condition)

micro_skills.definition: Produce defining and non-defining relative clauses correctly, using appropriate relative pronouns (who, which, that, whose, where) and punctuation.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Relative clauses are a primary GRA range indicator. Errors (wrong pronoun, missing comma for non-defining) are visible and penalised.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of relative clause errors
Proportion of relative clauses that are correct

2_min.title: Relative Pronoun Selection
2_min.instruction: Fill in 6 relative pronouns in given sentences.

5_min.title: Combine with Relative Clause
5_min.instruction: Combine 4 sentence pairs using a relative clause.

10_min.title: Relative Clause Audit
10_min.instruction: Find and correct all relative clause errors in your last essay.

15_min.title: Relative Clause-Rich Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph with at least 3 relative clauses (at least one defining, one non-defining). Zero errors.

failure_patterns: 'The people which live there' (who)
'Governments, that are responsible...' (which for non-defining)
Missing comma before non-defining relative clause
strength_indicators: Correct relative pronoun chosen based on animacy and defining status
Non-defining clauses consistently comma-delimited

micro_skills.definition: Produce first, second, and third conditional structures correctly, selecting the appropriate type for the intended meaning (real, hypothetical, or counterfactual).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Conditional structures are required for argument, prediction, and recommendation. Wrong conditional selection produces both meaning errors and GRA penalties.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of conditional structure errors
Proportion of conditionals using the correct type for their meaning

2_min.title: Conditional Type Sort
2_min.instruction: Label 4 conditional sentences as first, second, or third conditional.

5_min.title: Conditional Repair
5_min.instruction: Correct 3 conditional errors in given sentences.

10_min.title: Conditional Production
10_min.instruction: Write one conditional sentence for each type (1st, 2nd, 3rd) on the same topic.
10_min.success_criterion: All 3 grammatically correct and appropriate type

15_min.title: Conditional-Rich Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a recommendation paragraph using all 3 conditional types appropriately.

failure_patterns: 'If governments will invest...' (should: If governments invest/invested)
'If I would have known...' (should: If I had known)
Second conditional used for real/likely situations
strength_indicators: Conditional type matches the intended meaning (real vs. hypothetical)
No 'would' in the if-clause

micro_skills.definition: Produce grammatically correct comparative and superlative structures, including parallel comparisons and than/as constructions.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Comparison structures are high-frequency in data description and argumentation. Errors are penalised under both GRA and LR.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of errors in comparative/superlative structures
Proportion of comparison structures that are grammatically correct

2_min.title: Comparison Error Hunt
2_min.instruction: Find and correct 4 comparison structure errors.

5_min.title: Comparison Structure Variety
5_min.instruction: Express the same comparison in 4 different grammatical structures.

10_min.title: Chart Comparison Grammar
10_min.instruction: Write 5 comparison sentences from a given dataset. Vary comparison structures.
10_min.success_criterion: All accurate in data and grammar

15_min.title: Comparison-Dense Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph with 5+ comparison structures. Target zero grammar errors.

failure_patterns: 'more higher' (double comparative)
'the most highest' (double superlative)
'as bigger as' (should be 'as big as')
Non-parallel comparison: 'GDP of France vs. Italy's economy'
strength_indicators: All comparatives and superlatives formed correctly
Comparison structures parallel on both sides of than/as

micro_skills.definition: Express ideas in language that is immediately understandable without ambiguity, unnecessary complexity, or information overload per sentence.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Unclear writing forces the reader to re-read or guess intended meaning. Clarity failures undermine all other writing quality.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of sentences requiring re-reading to understand
Proportion of sentences that are immediately clear

2_min.title: Clarity Repair
2_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 unclear sentences so they communicate one clear idea each.

5_min.title: Sentence Simplification
5_min.instruction: Split 3 overloaded sentences (containing 2+ ideas) into 2 clear sentences each.
5_min.success_criterion: Each sentence contains exactly one idea

10_min.title: Clarity Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. Mark every sentence you had to re-read. Rewrite 3.

15_min.title: Maximum Clarity Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph targeting maximum clarity: one idea per sentence, unambiguous reference, precise vocabulary.

failure_patterns: Sentences with 3+ embedded clauses and multiple referents
Pronoun with no clear antecedent mid-complex sentence
Abstract noun stacking: 'the implementation of the development of educational provision'
strength_indicators: Every sentence communicates one clear proposition
No sentence requires re-reading to determine its subject

micro_skills.definition: Express ideas using the minimum number of words required without sacrificing meaning or completeness.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Wordy writing wastes word count, dilutes argument, and signals low writing efficiency. Conciseness is explicitly valued in academic and business writing.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of redundant words or phrases detected (e.g., 'in today's modern world', 'due to the fact that')
Average words used to express one complete idea (lower is better, within reason)

2_min.title: Word Cut Drill
2_min.instruction: Cut 5 wordy phrases to their concise equivalent. ('due to the fact that' → 'because')

5_min.title: Sentence Compression
5_min.instruction: Reduce 3 sentences by ≥ 30% without removing any key information.

10_min.title: Filler Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for filler phrases, redundant adverbs, and empty openers. List all.

15_min.title: Concise Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last body paragraph cutting 20% of words without removing any argument.
15_min.success_criterion: Word count ≥ 20% lower; no content lost

failure_patterns: 'In today's modern world of today...'
'due to the fact that' (because)
'It is important to note that...' (remove)
Padding final sentences to reach word count
strength_indicators: No phrase can be removed without reducing meaning
Sentences are direct; no throat-clearing openers

micro_skills.definition: Produce text that a target reader can process at a comfortable pace without cognitive overload, through appropriate sentence length variation and vocabulary selection.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Readability is a composite of clarity, conciseness, and sentence variety. Poor readability is felt by the examiner even when individual features are technically correct.

evaluation_metrics.description: Mean words per sentence (target: 18–25 for academic writing)
Std deviation of sentence lengths (target: 5–12)

2_min.title: Length Check
2_min.instruction: Count words in each of your last 5 sentences. Calculate average. Target 18–25.

5_min.title: Vary the Length
5_min.instruction: Rewrite a sequence of 4 sentences that are all the same length by varying each.

10_min.title: Readability Rewrite
10_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph that is all long sentences by breaking 2 into shorter ones.

15_min.title: Rhythmic Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph deliberately varying sentence length: short, long, medium, long, short.
15_min.success_criterion: Length pattern visible; all sentences clear

failure_patterns: Every sentence 35+ words
Short choppy sentences without variety
Single sentence paragraph
strength_indicators: Sentence lengths vary from ~10 to ~30 words
Paragraphs flow at natural reading pace

micro_skills.definition: Maintain a consistently appropriate level of formality in tone, sentence construction, and vocabulary across the entire text.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Formality inconsistency — formal vocabulary alongside informal tone — signals lack of stylistic control and is penalised in academic and professional writing.

evaluation_metrics.description: Degree of formality consistency across the essay
Number of sentences that violate the target formality level

2_min.title: Formality Violation Spotter
2_min.instruction: Identify 4 formality violations in a mixed-register paragraph.

5_min.title: Formality Calibration
5_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph to achieve consistent formal academic register throughout.

10_min.title: Formality Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for formality inconsistencies. List each violation with formal replacement.

15_min.title: Register-Consistent Essay
15_min.instruction: Write a full introduction and body paragraph at consistent formal academic register.
15_min.success_criterion: Zero formality violations across both sections

failure_patterns: One colloquial sentence in an otherwise formal essay
Formal vocabulary with informal syntactic structures
Mixing contractions with academic vocabulary
strength_indicators: Register is uniform across introduction, body, and conclusion
Tone does not shift between paragraphs

micro_skills.definition: Maintain an impersonal, objective, evidence-based tone appropriate to academic writing, avoiding emotional language, rhetorical questions, and direct address.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Non-academic tone is an LR and style penalty. Emotional or conversational tone indicates inability to produce academic register.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of non-academic tone markers detected (emotional language, rhetorical questions, direct address)
Proportion of sentences maintaining academic tone

2_min.title: Tone Violation Sort
2_min.instruction: Label 6 sentences as academic or non-academic tone.

5_min.title: Detachment Rewrite
5_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 emotionally charged sentences in academic objective tone.

10_min.title: Tone Audit
10_min.instruction: Flag every non-academic tone marker in your last essay. Correct each.

15_min.title: Academic Tone Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph on a topic you feel strongly about. Maintain full academic objectivity.

failure_patterns: 'Isn't it obvious that...' (rhetorical question)
'You can see that...' (direct address)
'It is absolutely shocking that...' (emotional language)
Exclamation marks
strength_indicators: No rhetorical questions
No direct address to reader
Emotional language replaced with analytical language

micro_skills.definition: Use sentence structure, word order, and lexical choice to foreground the most important information without over-amplifying every point.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who emphasise everything emphasise nothing. Selective emphasis guides the reader to key points and improves argumentative impact.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of sentences using unearned emphasis markers (very important, crucial, absolutely critical) for minor points
Proportion of key argumentative points that receive structural or lexical emphasis

2_min.title: Emphasis Calibration
2_min.instruction: Given 5 sentences, select which ONE deserves emphasis. Rewrite it with an emphasis structure.
2_min.success_criterion: Most important sentence selected; emphasis grammatically correct

5_min.title: Overemphasis Removal
5_min.instruction: Remove all emphasis markers from a paragraph that over-emphasises. Rewrite 1 to keep genuine emphasis.
5_min.success_criterion: Reduced to 1 genuine emphasis; others removed

10_min.title: End-Weight Practice
10_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 sentences to place the most important information at the end.
10_min.success_criterion: Key information consistently at sentence end

15_min.title: Emphasis Map
15_min.instruction: Read your last essay. Mark which point in each paragraph is most important. Check whether structure foregrounds it.
15_min.success_criterion: Key points identified; structural alignment assessed

failure_patterns: Every sentence contains 'very important' or 'crucial'
Most important point buried in the middle of a sentence
Conclusion emphasises minor examples instead of main argument
strength_indicators: Key claims appear in prominent sentence positions (end or start)
Emphasis markers reserved for genuinely important points

micro_skills.definition: Use modal verbs, adverbs, and epistemic phrases to appropriately qualify the certainty of claims, matching hedge strength to the evidence available.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Absent hedging produces overconfident claims. Excessive hedging produces meaningless assertions. Calibrated hedging is a marker of academic sophistication.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of hedges whose strength matches the evidence supporting the claim
Proportion of empirical claims with no hedging (lower is better where hedging is warranted)

2_min.title: Hedge Strength Match
2_min.instruction: Match 4 claims to the appropriate hedge: 'may', 'might', 'could', 'is likely to', 'appears to'.

5_min.title: Add Appropriate Hedges
5_min.instruction: Add appropriate hedges to 4 unhedged empirical claims.
5_min.success_criterion: All hedges appropriate to certainty level

10_min.title: Hedging Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay's empirical claims. Mark each as appropriately hedged, under-hedged, or over-hedged.

15_min.title: Hedging-Calibrated Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph with 3 claims at different certainty levels. Apply appropriate hedges to each.

failure_patterns: 'It is certain that...' for an opinion claim
'This might possibly perhaps suggest...' (over-hedged)
No hedging on debatable empirical claims
strength_indicators: Modal verb strength matches evidence quality
Different hedge strengths used across claims of varying certainty

micro_skills.definition: Vary sentence structures (simple, compound, complex, fronted adverbials, passive constructions, nominalisations) to avoid monotony and demonstrate grammatical range.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Structural monotony signals limited GRA range. Sentence variety is an explicit GRA descriptor at Band 7+.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of distinct sentence structure types used in essay
Proportion of consecutive sentence pairs using the same structure (lower is better)

2_min.title: Structure Identification
2_min.instruction: Label 6 sentences by structure type: simple, compound, complex, compound-complex, fronted, passive.

5_min.title: Structure Transformation
5_min.instruction: Rewrite the same idea in 4 different sentence structures.

10_min.title: Variety Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. List the structure of each sentence. Identify monotonous sequences.

15_min.title: Structure-Varied Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph where no two consecutive sentences share the same structure.

failure_patterns: Every sentence is Subject + Verb + Object (SVO monotony)
All sentences begin with 'It is' or 'There are'
No passive constructions in academic writing context
strength_indicators: Mix of fronted adverbials, passive, relative clauses, simple, and complex sentences
No two consecutive sentences have the same opening structure

micro_skills.definition: Produce grammatically correct compound sentences joining two independent clauses with coordinating conjunctions (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so) or a semicolon.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Compound sentences are a distinct structural type contributing to GRA range. They also enable contrastive and additive argument development.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of compound sentences that are grammatically correct
Number of distinct coordinating conjunctions used

2_min.title: Join With FANBOYS
2_min.instruction: Join 4 sentence pairs using 4 different coordinating conjunctions.
2_min.success_criterion: All 4 grammatically correct; all different conjunctions

5_min.title: Comma + Conjunction Drill
5_min.instruction: Write 3 compound sentences. Verify comma placement before each conjunction.

10_min.title: Compound vs. Complex Sort
10_min.instruction: Identify which of 8 sentences are compound and which are complex. Justify.

15_min.title: Structure-Variety Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph using simple, compound, and complex sentences in deliberate sequence.

failure_patterns: Comma splice used instead of compound sentence
Coordinating conjunction without comma for long clauses
Overuse of 'and' as the only conjunction
strength_indicators: Variety of coordinating conjunctions
Correct comma placement

micro_skills.definition: Produce at least one arguable, disputable proposition that directly responds to the prompt and serves as the central argumentative commitment of the essay or paragraph.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: No claim = no argument. Claim generation is the first bottleneck in argumentation. Writers who produce only descriptions or questions rather than propositions cannot score above Band 5 on TR.

evaluation_metrics.description: At least one arguable claim present in essay
Number of distinct claims per body paragraph
Proportion of identified claims that are genuinely disputable (not facts, definitions, or tautologies)

strength_indicators: Every body paragraph opens with a disputable proposition
Claims are distinct from facts and observable descriptions
failure_indicators: Paragraphs contain only examples with no governing claim
Topic sentence is a question
Claim is a restatement of the prompt

2_min.title: Claim vs. Non-Claim Sort
2_min.instruction: Label 6 sentences: claim / fact / question / description.

5_min.title: Generate 3 Claims
5_min.instruction: For a given prompt, generate 3 distinct arguable claims. No two may address the same aspect.

10_min.title: Claim Ladder
10_min.instruction: For a given topic, produce claims at 3 levels of specificity: broad position → sub-claim → specific proposition.
10_min.success_criterion: Each level is more specific than the previous

15_min.title: Claim-Per-Paragraph Outline
15_min.instruction: Write a full essay outline. Each body paragraph must open with one arguable claim. Justify arguability of each.
15_min.success_criterion: All claims disputable; all paragraph claims distinct

micro_skills.definition: Formulate claims that identify a specific subject, condition, scope, or mechanism rather than making broad, content-free assertions.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Generic claims ('technology is important') are unfalsifiable and unsupportable. Specific claims drive focused paragraphs and enable precise evidence selection.

evaluation_metrics.description: 0=generic assertion, 0.5=domain-specific, 1.0=specific subject+condition+scope
Proportion of claims that are generic/content-free (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Claims name specific agents, conditions, or mechanisms
Different prompts produce observably different claim content
failure_indicators: 'Technology has many advantages' type claims
Claims recycled across multiple different prompts
Claim does not constrain what counts as supporting evidence

2_min.title: Specificity Upgrade Drill
2_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 generic claims by adding subject + condition + scope.

5_min.title: Specificity Spectrum
5_min.instruction: For one topic, write 3 versions of the same claim at increasing specificity levels.

10_min.title: Specific Claim → Evidence Test
10_min.instruction: Write a specific claim. Then check: does it constrain exactly what evidence would support it? Verify.
10_min.success_criterion: Claim clearly implies what evidence is relevant

15_min.title: Specificity Audit
15_min.instruction: Audit last essay's claims. Score each for specificity. Rewrite any scoring < 0.5.

micro_skills.definition: Formulate claims with exact and unambiguous language so that the proposition has a single, determinable meaning — no vagueness, no hidden equivocation.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Imprecise claims shift meaning across the paragraph, making it impossible to maintain argumentative consistency. Precision is a prerequisite for coherent multi-step argument.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of claims containing ambiguous key terms
Proportion of claims with single-interpretation key terms

strength_indicators: Key terms in claims have single meanings in context
Claim meaning is stable across the whole paragraph
failure_indicators: 'Society' used without specifying which society
'People' claim that shifts between adults, youth, all humans mid-paragraph
Equivocation: using 'freedom' to mean both civil liberty and personal choice

2_min.title: Ambiguity Detection
2_min.instruction: Identify the ambiguous term in 4 given claims. Suggest a precise replacement.

5_min.title: Precision Rewrite
5_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 imprecise claims. Replace all vague key terms with precise alternatives.
5_min.success_criterion: All key terms have single determinable meanings

10_min.title: Term Stability Check
10_min.instruction: Take your last essay's main claim. Trace the key term through every sentence that uses it. Does it mean the same thing each time?

15_min.title: Precision-First Draft
15_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph where you define each key term in the claim before developing the argument.
15_min.success_criterion: Key terms defined; usage consistent throughout

micro_skills.definition: Ensure every claim made is directly relevant to the prompt's central question and advances the essay's overall argumentative position.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Relevant claims are distinct from focused claims — a claim can be specific but about a tangential topic. Relevance tracks the prompt; focus tracks the paragraph.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of claims traceable to the prompt's central question
Claims that are accurate but not relevant to the prompt (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Every claim contains prompt keyword or semantic equivalent
Removing any claim would weaken the response to the prompt
failure_indicators: Claim is true but addresses a different question
Claim is about a related topic not asked about
Body paragraph claim doesn't connect to thesis

2_min.title: Relevance Trace
2_min.instruction: For 4 given claims, trace each back to the prompt question. Mark as relevant/tangential.

5_min.title: Relevance Alignment
5_min.instruction: For each claim in your last essay, write one sentence explaining how it advances the answer to the prompt.
5_min.success_criterion: All explanations make a direct logical connection

10_min.title: Tangential Claim Removal
10_min.instruction: Identify any tangential claims in your last essay. Remove them and check the argument still holds.

15_min.title: Claim Relevance Map
15_min.instruction: Draw a map: prompt question → thesis → each body claim. Show logical chain from prompt to each claim.
15_min.success_criterion: All claims connected to prompt via at least 2 logical steps

micro_skills.definition: Produce at least one logical reason that explains WHY a claim is true — not an example of the claim, not a restatement, but a warrant-bearing explanatory proposition.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Claim without reason is assertion. The claim→reason step is the core of analytical writing and the most common missing element in Band 5–6 essays.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of claims followed by at least one reason
Reasons are logically distinct from their claims (not paraphrases)

strength_indicators: Every claim is followed by a 'because' or 'this is because' sentence
Reason adds logical content not present in the claim
failure_indicators: Claim → example (reason skipped)
Reason is a restatement of the claim in different vocabulary
'This is important' used as a reason

2_min.title: Because Drill
2_min.instruction: Complete: 'Claim X is true because...' for 4 different claims.
2_min.success_criterion: All add genuine logical content beyond the claim

5_min.title: Reason vs. Example Sort
5_min.instruction: Given 8 sentences following a claim, label each as reason or example.

10_min.title: Multi-Reason Generation
10_min.instruction: For one claim, generate 3 distinct reasons. Verify each is logically independent.
10_min.success_criterion: All 3 distinct; all logically support claim

15_min.title: Reason-Led Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a body paragraph: claim → reason → evidence → link. Reason must appear before evidence.

micro_skills.definition: Produce reasons that are logically valid (the reason actually explains why the claim is true), non-circular, and sufficient to move the argument forward.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A present but invalid reason is worse than no reason — it creates a logical flaw that undermines the entire paragraph.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of reasons that are logically valid supports for their claims
Reasons that restate the claim without adding logical content (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Reasons contain a mechanism or process that explains the causal link to the claim
Reasons survive the 'so what?' challenge — they independently justify the claim
failure_indicators: 'Social media is harmful because it has a negative effect on people' (circular)
Reason is true but explains a different claim
Reason assumes the conclusion

2_min.title: Circular Reason Detector
2_min.instruction: Identify which of 4 reasons are circular. Rewrite each.
2_min.success_criterion: All circular reasons identified; rewrites are valid

5_min.title: Reason Validity Test
5_min.instruction: For each of 3 reasons, apply: 'Does this reason actually explain why the claim is true, or does it assume it?' Rate each.

10_min.title: Mechanism Reason
10_min.instruction: Rewrite 2 weak reasons to include a causal mechanism (the process by which the reason makes the claim true).

15_min.title: Reason Audit
15_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. Rate each reason for validity. Rewrite the weakest.

micro_skills.definition: Ensure that each reason is relevant to the specific claim it follows — not to a related claim, not to the general topic, but to this specific proposition.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers frequently produce reasons that are true and relevant to the topic but not to the specific claim they are supposed to support, causing argumentative drift.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of reasons that are relevant to their immediate claim (not just the topic)

strength_indicators: Each reason contains vocabulary from or directly derived from its claim
No reason could be transplanted to a different claim without modification
failure_indicators: Reason is about the general topic but doesn't support the specific claim
Reason supports a related but different claim than the one stated

2_min.title: Reason-Claim Match
2_min.instruction: Match 4 reasons to 4 claims from a shuffled list.

5_min.title: Drift Detection
5_min.instruction: In a given paragraph, identify where the reason drifts from supporting the claim to discussing a related topic.

10_min.title: Relevance Repair
10_min.instruction: Rewrite a reason that drifts from its claim so that it stays tightly connected.
10_min.success_criterion: Reason contains claim vocabulary; stays on-claim

15_min.title: Claim-Locked Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph where every sentence traces back to the opening claim. Annotate each sentence with what part of the claim it develops.

micro_skills.definition: Produce concrete supporting material (data, examples, cases, statistics, documented facts) that gives empirical grounding to a claim-reason pair.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Support converts argument from assertion to demonstration. Without it, even valid reasoning produces unpersuasive writing.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of claim-reason pairs with at least one support item
0=none, 0.3=vague reference, 0.6=named domain, 1.0=specific datum/case/statistic

strength_indicators: Support items contain named entities, quantified data, or situated cases
Every paragraph has at least one concrete support item
failure_indicators: 'Studies show...' with no specifics
Support is another assertion, not empirical grounding
Same support item used in multiple paragraphs

2_min.title: Support Concreteness Upgrade
2_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 vague support items to concrete. Add name/number/location.

5_min.title: Support Bank
5_min.instruction: For 3 common essay topics, generate 2 specific support items each (named cases, data points).

10_min.title: Support Integration
10_min.instruction: For your last essay's weakest paragraph, generate 2 concrete support items and integrate them.
10_min.success_criterion: Both support items concrete and integrated

15_min.title: Evidence-Dense Argument
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph with: claim → reason → 2 concrete support items → analytical link. Each support must be specific.
15_min.success_criterion: Both support items rated concrete; all elements present

micro_skills.definition: Verify that each support item directly and logically corroborates the specific claim-reason pair it is placed under, rather than supporting a related but different proposition.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Misaligned support is the most common evidence error in Band 5–6 writing. It creates an illusion of argument while the logic breaks down at the claim–support junction.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of support items that directly corroborate their stated claim-reason pair
Support items that illustrate a different claim than stated (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Support item vocabulary semantically overlaps with claim vocabulary
Post-support analytical sentence explicitly connects support back to claim
failure_indicators: Support illustrates a different point than the stated claim
Support is placed in wrong paragraph
No analytical link sentence connecting support to claim

2_min.title: Alignment Check
2_min.instruction: Given 4 claim+support pairs, rate alignment: aligned / misaligned / partially.

5_min.title: Link Sentence Writing
5_min.instruction: For 3 given claim+support pairs, write one analytical link sentence that explicitly connects them.
5_min.success_criterion: All links reference both the claim and the support

10_min.title: Misalignment Repair
10_min.instruction: Find a misaligned support in your last essay. Either move it to the right paragraph or replace it.

15_min.title: Full Alignment Audit
15_min.instruction: For every claim-support pair in your last essay, rate alignment and add a link sentence to any rated < 0.7.

micro_skills.definition: Produce a concrete illustrative case that instantiates the abstract claim-reason pair, making the argument tangible and verifiable.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Examples make abstract arguments concrete. They are the most accessible form of support for readers and the primary vehicle for demonstrating real-world applicability.

evaluation_metrics.description: At least one example per body paragraph
0=hypothetical generic, 0.5=domain-specific, 1.0=named/dated/quantified

strength_indicators: Examples contain at least one of: named entity, statistic, country, date, industry
Example is a specific case, not a hypothetical
failure_indicators: 'For example, many people use social media...' (generic)
Example is a hypothetical scenario with no grounding
Example is the same as the claim restated concretely

2_min.title: Generic → Concrete
2_min.instruction: Upgrade 3 generic examples. Add: who, where, when, or how much.

5_min.title: Example Bank Sprint
5_min.instruction: Generate 2 concrete examples each for: technology, environment, education. 6 total.
5_min.success_criterion: All 6 concrete and relevant to topic

10_min.title: Example-Reason Fit
10_min.instruction: For 3 given reasons, generate one example that specifically illustrates that reason (not just the topic).
10_min.success_criterion: All 3 examples illustrate the specific reason, not just the topic

15_min.title: Full CREE Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write: Claim → Reason → Example (concrete) → Explanation of how example supports reason.
15_min.success_criterion: All 4 elements present; example is concrete; explanation links back

micro_skills.definition: Ensure that an example illustrates the specific claim-reason pair it is intended to support, not merely the general topic.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Topically relevant but argumentatively irrelevant examples are the most common example error. They inflate apparent support while providing none.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of examples that directly illustrate their stated reason/claim

strength_indicators: Example contains content that directly instantiates the reason
Link sentence explicitly states what the example proves
failure_indicators: Example is about the topic but doesn't illustrate the specific reason
Example could support multiple different claims equally well

2_min.title: Example-Reason Match
2_min.instruction: Match 4 examples to 4 reasons from a shuffled list.

5_min.title: Relevance Test
5_min.instruction: For 3 reason-example pairs, write 'What exactly does this example prove about this reason?' If you can't answer, the example is not relevant.
5_min.success_criterion: All answers directly reference the reason

10_min.title: Example Replacement
10_min.instruction: Identify one example in your last essay that is topically but not argumentatively relevant. Replace it.
10_min.success_criterion: New example directly illustrates the specific reason

15_min.title: Relevance Audit
15_min.instruction: For every example in your last essay, write one sentence explaining what specific aspect of the reason it illustrates.
15_min.success_criterion: All explanations reference the specific reason content

micro_skills.definition: Produce examples with sufficient specificity to be verifiable and persuasive — including named agents, quantified outcomes, situated contexts, or dated events.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Specificity determines example persuasive force. 'A country reduced emissions' is weaker than 'Finland reduced per-capita CO2 by 20% between 2000 and 2020'.

evaluation_metrics.description: 0=pure generic, 0.33=domain label, 0.66=named entity, 1.0=named+quantified or named+dated+situated
Examples with no specific anchoring details (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Examples contain at least one proper noun or quantified figure
Examples could be fact-checked in principle
failure_indicators: 'In many countries around the world...'
'Research has shown that...' with no citation
'For instance, some people...'

2_min.title: Specificity Scoring
2_min.instruction: Rate 5 given examples on the 0–1 specificity scale. Justify.

5_min.title: Add the Detail
5_min.instruction: For 3 generic examples, add: (a) a name, (b) a number or date, (c) a location or outcome.

10_min.title: Personal Example Bank
10_min.instruction: Build a bank of 8 specific examples across 4 topics. Each must rate ≥ 0.66.

15_min.title: Specificity Upgrade Essay
15_min.instruction: Take your last essay. Upgrade all generic examples to specific. Verify all rate ≥ 0.66.

micro_skills.definition: Embed an example into the paragraph so it is introduced, presented, and followed by an analytical link that explicitly connects it back to the claim-reason pair.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: An example dropped without framing disrupts flow. An example without post-example analysis leaves the argumentative work undone.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of examples with an introductory framing phrase
Proportion of examples followed by an analytical link to the claim

strength_indicators: Every example has: framing phrase + content + link sentence
Link sentence explicitly restates what the example demonstrates about the claim
failure_indicators: Example ends the paragraph with no analysis
Example dropped after claim with no framing
'This shows that...' with no specific reference to what it shows

2_min.title: Frame + Link Sprint
2_min.instruction: Add a framing phrase and a link sentence to 3 bare examples.
2_min.success_criterion: All framing phrases correct; all link sentences reference the reason

5_min.title: Integration Check
5_min.instruction: Rate 4 given examples on integration: frame present? analysis present? Both?

10_min.title: Full CRE-L Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write: Claim → Reason → [framed Example] → Link to reason. All 4 elements required.

15_min.title: Integration Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last essay's body paragraphs ensuring every example has framing and analysis.
15_min.success_criterion: All examples framed and followed by links

micro_skills.definition: Articulate the underlying principle or assumption that connects the reason to the claim — the unstated rule of inference that makes the argument valid.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Warrants are the missing logical bridges in most undergraduate and B2 writing. Making them explicit distinguishes transparent analytical reasoning from implicit assertion.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of arguments containing an explicit or clearly implied warrant
0=absent, 0.5=implied, 1.0=explicitly stated

strength_indicators: Arguments include a sentence of the form 'This matters because [general principle]'
The logical gap between reason and claim is explicitly bridged
failure_indicators: Reason and claim connected with no bridge principle
Reader must supply the missing logical step
Warrant assumed but never stated in a complex argument

2_min.title: Bridge the Gap
2_min.instruction: For 3 claim-reason pairs, write the missing warrant (the general principle that makes the reason support the claim).
2_min.success_criterion: All 3 correctly bridge claim and reason

5_min.title: Warrant vs. Reason
5_min.instruction: Given 6 sentences following a claim, label each as reason / warrant / example / link.

10_min.title: Explicit Warrant Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write an argument paragraph that explicitly states the warrant as a general principle.
10_min.success_criterion: Warrant identifiable as a general principle connecting reason to claim

15_min.title: Warrant Audit
15_min.instruction: For each argument in your last essay, identify the implied warrant. Write 1 sentence making it explicit. Insert into essay.
15_min.success_criterion: All warrants correctly identified and explicitly stated

micro_skills.definition: Produce warrants that are genuinely general, defensible principles — not restatements of the reason, not tautologies, and not more controversial than the claim they support.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A weak warrant introduces a new controversial premise that requires as much support as the original claim, creating argumentative regression.

evaluation_metrics.description: Warrant is a general principle, not tied to the specific case
Warrant is more widely accepted than the claim it supports

strength_indicators: Warrant could be removed from the essay and used to support a different argument on the same topic
Warrant is stated at higher level of abstraction than reason
failure_indicators: Warrant is as controversial as the claim
Warrant is just the reason paraphrased at a slightly higher level
Warrant introduces a new empirical claim requiring its own support

2_min.title: Warrant Quality Sort
2_min.instruction: Rate 4 warrants: strong (general + accepted) / weak (too specific or controversial).

5_min.title: Generalization Test
5_min.instruction: For 3 warrants, apply: 'Is this principle general enough to support multiple different specific claims?' If not, revise.

10_min.title: Controversy Audit
10_min.instruction: For each warrant in your last essay, rate: is this warrant less controversial than the claim? Fix any that aren't.
10_min.success_criterion: All warrants less controversial than claims

15_min.title: Strong Warrant Essay
15_min.instruction: Rewrite one argument using a strong, general, accepted warrant as an explicit bridge sentence.
15_min.success_criterion: Warrant is general, accepted, and correctly bridges reason to claim

micro_skills.definition: Generate the strongest available opposition to the essay's main claim — a well-grounded alternative position with its own supporting reason.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Generates intellectual engagement with the topic. Absent counterarguments are penalised in both IELTS and academic writing as one-dimensional thinking.

evaluation_metrics.description: At least one counterargument present
Counterargument represents a genuinely strong opposition (not strawman)

strength_indicators: Counterargument contains its own claim + reason (not just 'some people disagree')
Counterargument would persuade a neutral reader if left unanswered
failure_indicators: Counterargument is a weaker version of own claim
Strawman: misrepresents the opposing view
Counterargument is one clause: 'although others think differently'

2_min.title: Steel Man in 60 Seconds
2_min.instruction: For your last essay's main claim, write the strongest possible counter-claim in 2 sentences.

5_min.title: Three Counterarguments
5_min.instruction: Generate 3 distinct counterarguments to a given claim. Order them strongest to weakest.

10_min.title: Developed Counterargument
10_min.instruction: Write a counterargument paragraph: opposing claim + reason + one piece of support.

15_min.title: Best Counterargument Selection
15_min.instruction: Generate 4 counterarguments. Select the strongest. Write a full developed counterargument paragraph for it. Justify your selection.
15_min.success_criterion: Selected counterargument is strongest; paragraph fully developed

micro_skills.definition: Develop the counterargument to a sufficient logical depth (claim + reason + support) before rebutting, demonstrating intellectual fairness.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Shallow counterarguments ('some people think X') signal intellectual avoidance. Full development signals confidence in the main argument's ability to survive genuine challenge.

evaluation_metrics.description: Steps in counterargument chain: 1=bare claim, 2=claim+reason, 3=claim+reason+support
Words devoted to counterargument as share of body text (target: 0.15–0.30)

strength_indicators: Counterargument paragraph contains claim + reason + at least one support item
Counterargument is developed before rebuttal appears
failure_indicators: Rebuttal appears in the same sentence as the counterargument
Counterargument is one sentence long
Counterargument less developed than main argument paragraphs

2_min.title: Develop the Counter
2_min.instruction: Take a 1-sentence counterargument. Add a reason and a support item.

5_min.title: Word Count Balance
5_min.instruction: Compare word counts of your main argument paragraphs vs. counterargument. Calculate ratio.
5_min.success_criterion: Correct ratio calculated; imbalance identified if present

10_min.title: Full Counter Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a counterargument paragraph with claim + reason + support before any rebuttal language.

15_min.title: Counterargument + Rebuttal Essay Section
15_min.instruction: Write a full concession-rebuttal section: counterargument fully developed, then rebuttal.
15_min.success_criterion: Counter depth score = 1.0; rebuttal adds new content

micro_skills.definition: Produce a response to the counterargument that defends the main position using new logical content — not a simple reassertion of the original claim.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A rebuttal is the argumentative move that closes the concession loop. Without it, the essay appears to concede the opposing point.

evaluation_metrics.description: A rebuttal follows each counterargument
Rebuttal introduces new logical content not in the main argument (0=reassertion, 1=new content)

strength_indicators: Rebuttal contains a qualifier, a limitation of the counterargument's scope, or counter-evidence
Rebuttal is logically distinct from the main argument
failure_indicators: No rebuttal — essay ends after counterargument
'However, I still believe...' (reassertion with no new content)
Rebuttal only repeats the main claim in different words

2_min.title: Rebuttal Type
2_min.instruction: Label 4 rebuttals as: limitation, counter-evidence, qualification, or reassertion.

5_min.title: Limitation Rebuttal
5_min.instruction: Write a rebuttal that limits the counterargument's scope without denying it completely.

10_min.title: Evidence Rebuttal
10_min.instruction: Write a rebuttal using a specific piece of evidence that limits the counterargument's validity.
10_min.success_criterion: Evidence is specific and logically limits the counter

15_min.title: Counter + Rebuttal Sequence
15_min.instruction: Write a full concession-rebuttal passage. Rebuttal must use a different rhetorical move than main arguments (limit, qualify, or use counter-evidence).
15_min.success_criterion: Rebuttal rated as new-content by evaluator

micro_skills.definition: Assess whether the rebuttal logically addresses the specific points made in the counterargument (not the general opposing position) and advances the main argument.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A rebuttal that ignores the counterargument's specific content is a non-sequitur. Quality requires direct engagement with what was conceded.

evaluation_metrics.description: Rebuttal addresses the specific content of the counterargument (not the general topic)
Rebuttal logic is valid — the new content genuinely limits or overturns the counterargument

strength_indicators: Rebuttal vocabulary overlaps with counterargument vocabulary
Rebuttal identifies a specific condition under which the counterargument fails
failure_indicators: Rebuttal addresses the general topic, not the specific counterargument
Rebuttal is logically non-sequitur to the counter
Rebuttal concedes the full counterargument while claiming to refute it

2_min.title: Rebuttal Relevance Check
2_min.instruction: For 3 counter+rebuttal pairs, check: does the rebuttal address the specific counterargument or the general topic?

5_min.title: Specific Counter-Rebuttal
5_min.instruction: Take a counterargument. Write a rebuttal that references at least 2 specific words from the counterargument.
5_min.success_criterion: Both specific references present; logic valid

10_min.title: Condition-Based Rebuttal
10_min.instruction: Write a rebuttal that identifies the specific conditions under which the counterargument holds, then argues those conditions are rare or do not apply here.
10_min.success_criterion: Conditions specified; argument limits counterargument scope

15_min.title: Quality Rebuttal Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Take a weak rebuttal from your last essay. Rewrite it to achieve specificity score ≥ 0.8 and logical validity.

micro_skills.definition: Maintain a consistent argumentative position across the entire essay — thesis, body arguments, counterargument concession, and conclusion must all be logically compatible.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Position drift is one of the most common TR failures at B2+. The essay takes one position in the introduction and a different (or incompatible) one in the conclusion.

evaluation_metrics.description: Degree to which thesis, body claims, and conclusion support the same position
Number of paragraphs where the stated position is incompatible with the thesis

strength_indicators: Thesis position is traceable through every body paragraph
Conclusion restates thesis without introducing new or incompatible position
failure_indicators: Thesis says 'agree' but conclusion says 'both sides have merit'
Concession so strong it overturns the thesis without recovery
Body arguments support a different position than the stated thesis

2_min.title: Position Trace
2_min.instruction: Label each paragraph of your last essay with the position it supports. Are they all consistent?
2_min.success_criterion: All consistent labels identified; inconsistencies flagged

5_min.title: Thesis-Conclusion Alignment
5_min.instruction: Read only your introduction and conclusion. Write one sentence describing each position. Are they the same?

10_min.title: Drift Detection + Fix
10_min.instruction: Identify any paragraph where the position drifts from the thesis. Rewrite it to restore consistency.

15_min.title: Position-Consistent Essay
15_min.instruction: Write a 4-paragraph essay. After each paragraph, check: does this advance my thesis? Only move to next paragraph when answer is yes.

micro_skills.definition: Ensure the conclusion follows logically from the body arguments and restates the position earned by the argument — not a stronger or weaker position than the evidence supports.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Conclusions that overclaim (stronger than argument supports) or underclaim (weaker than argument supports) create logical incoherence and undermine TR.

evaluation_metrics.description: Degree to which conclusion position matches what body arguments actually established
Conclusion asserts a stronger position than body arguments support

strength_indicators: Conclusion vocabulary echoes argument vocabulary, not new claims
Conclusion uses hedging proportional to evidence strength in body
failure_indicators: Conclusion: 'Therefore technology is the solution to all modern problems' (overclaim)
Conclusion: 'In conclusion, there are advantages and disadvantages' (underclaims own argument)
Conclusion introduces a new argument not in the body

2_min.title: Overclaim Detector
2_min.instruction: Label 4 conclusions as: aligned / overclaim / underclaim.

5_min.title: Conclusion-Body Match
5_min.instruction: Read your body arguments. Write a 1-sentence summary of what they collectively prove. Write a conclusion that matches exactly that.
5_min.success_criterion: Conclusion scope matches body argument scope

10_min.title: Evidence-Calibrated Conclusion
10_min.instruction: Rewrite your conclusion with hedging proportional to your actual evidence strength.

15_min.title: Argument Audit → Conclusion
15_min.instruction: Map all body arguments. Identify the strongest and weakest. Write a conclusion that accurately summarises what was established.

micro_skills.definition: Each communicative unit (sentence or clause) expresses exactly one proposition with unambiguous subject, predicate, and logical scope.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Unclear propositions prevent the evaluator from determining what the writer is claiming. A sentence that could mean two things means neither reliably.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of sentences containing ambiguous or double propositions
Proportion of sentences with a single, determinable proposition

strength_indicators: Every sentence expresses exactly one proposition
All subjects and predicates are unambiguous in context
failure_indicators: Sentence that contains two incompatible propositions
Subject referent unclear — proposition cannot be evaluated
Compound sentence where each clause contradicts the other

2_min.title: One Proposition Per Sentence
2_min.instruction: Split 3 multi-proposition sentences into separate clear sentences.
2_min.success_criterion: Each resulting sentence has one proposition

5_min.title: Clarity Test
5_min.instruction: For 5 sentences, write 'what exactly is being claimed?' If you can't answer, the proposition is unclear.

10_min.title: Proposition Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay. Mark every sentence where the proposition is unclear or double.

15_min.title: Single-Proposition Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last essay's most complex paragraph ensuring every sentence has exactly one clear proposition.

micro_skills.definition: Every proposition in the essay is fully stated — subject, predicate, and necessary modifiers are present; no critical logical element is assumed without being stated.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Incomplete propositions require the reader to supply missing content, creating comprehension burden and introducing unintended interpretations.

evaluation_metrics.description: Sentences where a critical element (who, what, to whom, under what condition) is missing

strength_indicators: Every sentence has an identifiable agent, action, and scope
Modifying conditions are stated, not assumed
failure_indicators: 'This affects people' — which people, how, under what conditions?
'It is important' — what is important, important for whom?
Sentence with implied subject that could refer to two different entities

2_min.title: Complete the Proposition
2_min.instruction: Add missing elements to 4 incomplete propositions.
2_min.success_criterion: All completions add genuine missing information

5_min.title: Missing Element Hunt
5_min.instruction: In a given paragraph, identify every sentence missing who, what, or under-what-condition.

10_min.title: Completeness Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for incomplete propositions.

15_min.title: Full Proposition Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph where every sentence is explicitly complete. No assumed subjects, objects, or conditions.

micro_skills.definition: Verify that each paragraph contains a complete logical chain: Claim → Reason → Support → Example → (Counterargument if applicable) → Conclusion link — with no steps missing.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Missing chain steps are the primary cause of shallow writing at B2. Completeness is measurable from essay structure and is the most direct predictor of argument depth.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of required chain steps present per paragraph (averaged across all body paragraphs)
Which steps are most frequently absent (claim / reason / support / example / link)

strength_indicators: Every body paragraph contains all 4 core steps: claim, reason, support/example, link
missing_step_profile shows 0 missing across all steps
failure_indicators: Reason consistently missing — claim jumps to example
Link sentence always absent — examples float without analytical closure
Support present but claim absent — paragraph starts with evidence

2_min.title: Chain Completeness Check
2_min.instruction: For 2 given paragraphs, tick which steps are present: C, R, S, E, L. Calculate completeness %.

5_min.title: Add the Missing Step
5_min.instruction: Identify the missing step in 3 given paragraphs. Write the missing sentence.

10_min.title: Chain Map
10_min.instruction: Map your last essay's every paragraph against the C-R-S-E-L chain. Calculate mean completeness.

15_min.title: Complete Chain Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write one body paragraph with all 5 steps explicitly present. Label each step.

micro_skills.definition: Structurally verify that the conclusion paragraph performs three functions: (1) restates thesis, (2) synthesises body arguments, (3) closes with a position-consistent final statement.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Structural completeness of the conclusion is directly measurable and is one of the most consistent predictors of Band 6 vs. Band 7 in IELTS TR and CC.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of 3 required conclusion functions present
No new arguments introduced in conclusion (1=clean, 0=new content)

strength_indicators: All 3 functions identifiable in conclusion paragraph
No new arguments or evidence in conclusion
failure_indicators: Conclusion only restates thesis — synthesis missing
Conclusion introduces new argument
Conclusion's position is weaker or stronger than body argument establishes

2_min.title: Conclusion Function Label
2_min.instruction: Label each sentence of 3 model conclusions: thesis-restatement / synthesis / closing-statement / new-content.

5_min.title: 3-Function Conclusion
5_min.instruction: Write a conclusion containing all 3 functions in 3 sentences.

10_min.title: Missing Function Repair
10_min.instruction: Identify which function(s) are missing from your last conclusion. Add them.

15_min.title: Full Structural Essay Close
15_min.instruction: Write a conclusion that scores 3/3 on function coverage and 1 on no-new-content. Annotate each sentence.

micro_skills.definition: Construct and evaluate causal chains by identifying sufficient causes, necessary conditions, mechanisms, and potential confounds — independently of how the reasoning is expressed in writing.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: The D3 causal_reasoning skill measures whether the writer expressed a valid causal claim in text. This skill measures whether the writer can construct a valid causal model before writing — a thinking competence that may or may not translate into the essay.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who cannot reason causally will never produce valid causal arguments, regardless of linguistic coaching. This is the upstream bottleneck.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by auditing the logical structure of causal claims: are causes genuinely prior to effects? Are mechanisms plausible? Are confounds acknowledged?

evaluation_metrics.description: Maximum length of valid causal chain produced
Proportion of causal claims that acknowledge potential confounds or limitations
Proportion of causes where the mechanism is explicitly stated

strength_indicators: Causal chains include mechanism sentences
Confounds or limitations noted with hedging
Cause is logically prior to effect
failure_indicators: Correlation stated as causation
Reverse causation undetected
No mechanism — just assertion of causal link

2_min.title: Causal vs. Correlational
2_min.instruction: Label 4 statements as causal, correlational, or ambiguous.

5_min.title: Mechanism Chain
5_min.instruction: For one cause-effect pair, write a 3-step mechanism chain explaining HOW the cause produces the effect.

10_min.title: Confound Identification
10_min.instruction: For 2 given causal claims, identify at least one confound for each.

15_min.title: Full Causal Model
15_min.instruction: For a given phenomenon, build a full causal model: 2 causes → mechanisms → effect → 2nd-order effects → 1 confound.

micro_skills.definition: Identify the relevant dimensions on which two or more entities differ, determine which differences are meaningful for the purpose at hand, and produce a valid comparative judgment.
