micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: generate_comparisons (D2) measures whether the writer can produce comparison content. This skill measures the underlying cognitive operation: can the writer identify the right dimensions to compare and weigh them appropriately?
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Superficial comparisons ('Country A is richer than Country B') without relevant dimension analysis produce low-quality arguments in both academic and data tasks.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by checking: are comparison dimensions stated? Are the dimensions relevant to the argument? Is the comparison conclusion proportional to the evidence?

evaluation_metrics.description: Comparisons state the dimension being compared (not just the direction)
Comparison conclusion is proportional to evidence

strength_indicators: Comparison sentences specify what dimension is being compared
Comparative conclusions are hedged proportionally to evidence
failure_indicators: 'A is better than B' with no dimension specified
Comparison of non-comparable entities
Comparison conclusion stronger than evidence supports

2_min.title: Dimension Naming
2_min.instruction: For 4 vague comparisons, name the dimension being compared.

5_min.title: Dimension Selection
5_min.instruction: For a given argument, choose the 2 most relevant dimensions on which to compare 2 options.

10_min.title: Comparative Reasoning Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph comparing 2 approaches on 2 dimensions. Conclude which is better and why.

15_min.title: Multi-Dimensional Comparison
15_min.instruction: Compare 3 options on 3 dimensions in a structured paragraph. Identify which option is best for the given purpose.

micro_skills.definition: Identify and weigh competing values, costs, and benefits of a decision or policy, producing a reasoned judgment about which tradeoff position is most defensible given stated priorities.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: evaluation_of_alternatives (D3) measures whether the writer discusses multiple options. Tradeoff analysis measures whether the writer correctly identifies that choosing one option means sacrificing another — a deeper cognitive operation.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Essays that discuss advantages and disadvantages without identifying the tradeoff between them produce list-like content rather than analytical conclusions.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: (a) does the writer acknowledge a genuine cost of their preferred option? (b) is the tradeoff explicitly named? (c) is the conclusion justified by the tradeoff analysis?

evaluation_metrics.description: Writer correctly identifies what is sacrificed by choosing their preferred option
Writer provides reasoning for why one side of the tradeoff outweighs the other

strength_indicators: Essay explicitly names what is sacrificed by the preferred option
Conclusion uses language of weighing: 'while X is lost, Y is more important because...'
failure_indicators: Advantages and disadvantages listed without a weighting judgment
Preferred option has no acknowledged cost
Conclusion appears arbitrary — no explicit weighing

2_min.title: Name the Tradeoff
2_min.instruction: For 3 given policy decisions, name the core tradeoff (what is gained vs. what is sacrificed).

5_min.title: Weigh the Tradeoff
5_min.instruction: For a given tradeoff, write 2 sentences arguing why one side outweighs the other.

10_min.title: Tradeoff Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that: names the tradeoff → acknowledges the cost of your position → argues why the benefit outweighs the cost.

15_min.title: Tradeoff-Anchored Essay
15_min.instruction: Write a 4-paragraph essay where the central argument is structured as a tradeoff analysis.
15_min.success_criterion: Tradeoff named; cost acknowledged; weighing argument present

micro_skills.definition: Systematically trace first-order and second-order consequences of a cause or action, distinguishing intended from unintended consequences and immediate from long-term effects.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: generate_consequences (D2) produces consequence content. This thinking skill involves the systematic analytical process of tracing consequences across time horizons and stakeholder groups.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Essays that only state the obvious first-order consequence score lower on TR than those that trace second-order effects or identify unintended consequences.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: (a) number of consequence tiers (1st order, 2nd order), (b) presence of unintended consequences, (c) time horizon variety (short-term vs. long-term).

evaluation_metrics.description: Maximum consequence tier reached (1=first-order only, 2=second-order, 3=systemic)
Essay identifies at least one unintended consequence

strength_indicators: Essay traces consequences beyond the obvious first-order effect
At least one unintended or long-term consequence identified
failure_indicators: Only first-order consequences stated
All consequences intended and positive — no critical analysis
Consequences listed without time horizon differentiation

2_min.title: 2nd Order Chain
2_min.instruction: For a given action, identify: immediate consequence → second-order consequence.

5_min.title: Unintended Consequence
5_min.instruction: For a given policy, identify one unintended consequence that contradicts the policy's intent.

10_min.title: Consequence Map
10_min.instruction: Map: action → 2 first-order → 2 second-order consequences. Identify 1 unintended.
10_min.success_criterion: All steps valid; unintended consequence genuine

15_min.title: Consequence-Rich Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that develops 2 consequence tiers and acknowledges 1 unintended consequence.

micro_skills.definition: Generate and evaluate multiple candidate solutions to a problem, selecting and justifying the most effective based on causal alignment, feasibility, and scope.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: generate_solutions (D2) produces solution content for essays. This thinking skill involves the prior cognitive process of systematically generating and evaluating multiple solutions before selecting one to write about.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who only generate one solution produce essays that appear uncritical and often select suboptimal solutions because they haven't considered alternatives.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: number of distinct solutions offered, whether they address different causal factors, whether selection rationale is provided.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of distinct solutions addressing different causal factors
Writer provides reasoning for why the chosen solution is superior

strength_indicators: Multiple solutions proposed; each targets a different cause
Solution selection justified against alternatives
failure_indicators: Only one solution proposed
Solutions don't target identified causes
Solution selected without justification

2_min.title: 3-Solution Sprint
2_min.instruction: Generate 3 distinct solutions to a given problem in 2 minutes.

5_min.title: Solution Evaluation
5_min.instruction: Rate 3 given solutions on feasibility (0–1), causal alignment (0–1), scope (0–1).

10_min.title: Best Solution Selection
10_min.instruction: Generate 4 solutions. Select the best. Write 3 sentences justifying the selection against the alternatives.

15_min.title: Solution-Hierarchy Essay Section
15_min.instruction: Write a solutions section proposing 2 different solutions and justifying their relative priority.

micro_skills.definition: Systematically assess multiple competing positions, solutions, or interpretations against explicit criteria before reaching a conclusion.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: evaluation_of_alternatives (D3) measures whether the writer discusses alternatives. This skill measures the cognitive quality of the evaluation process — are the criteria explicit? Is the evaluation systematic?
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Apparent evaluation without explicit criteria is opinion masking as analysis. Systematic evaluation with stated criteria is the hallmark of B2+ analytical writing.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: (a) are evaluation criteria explicitly stated? (b) are all alternatives evaluated against the same criteria? (c) does the conclusion follow from the evaluation?

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of evaluations where criteria are explicitly stated
Same criteria applied to all alternatives being evaluated

strength_indicators: Evaluation criteria stated before alternatives are compared
Same criteria applied to all options
Conclusion explicitly references how the winner scored on the criteria
failure_indicators: Conclusion appears before criteria are stated
Different criteria applied to different alternatives
Evaluation criteria shift mid-comparison

2_min.title: Criteria First
2_min.instruction: For a given choice, state 3 evaluation criteria before naming any alternatives.

5_min.title: Matrix Evaluation
5_min.instruction: Evaluate 2 alternatives on 2 criteria using a simple 2x2 matrix.
5_min.success_criterion: Both alternatives evaluated on both criteria

10_min.title: Criterion-Based Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that states criteria, applies them to 2 options, and concludes.
10_min.success_criterion: Criteria explicit; applied consistently; conclusion follows

15_min.title: Evaluation-Structured Essay
15_min.instruction: Write an essay section where the evaluation structure is explicit: criteria → application → conclusion.

micro_skills.definition: Rank ideas, arguments, causes, solutions, or evidence items by their importance or impact, and structure the essay to reflect that ranking.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: select_relevant_information (D4) measures content selection from data. Prioritization is a thinking skill: can the writer determine which of their ideas is most important and structure the essay accordingly?
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Essays structured in order of discovery rather than importance bury the best arguments. Prioritization thinking directly translates to argument order and paragraph weighting.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: is the strongest argument in the most prominent paragraph? Does the essay sequence reflect importance ordering?

evaluation_metrics.description: Strongest argument appears in paragraph 2 or final body paragraph (most prominent positions)
Word count distribution reflects argument importance ranking

strength_indicators: Best argument given most space and prominent position
Weaker arguments correctly de-emphasised
failure_indicators: Random argument ordering with no apparent importance logic
Weakest argument placed first and given most space
All paragraphs equal length regardless of argument importance

2_min.title: Rank the Arguments
2_min.instruction: Given 4 arguments for a position, rank from strongest to weakest. Justify.

5_min.title: Importance-Ordered Outline
5_min.instruction: Create an essay outline with paragraphs ordered by argument strength, strongest first.

10_min.title: Reorder for Impact
10_min.instruction: Take your last essay's body paragraphs. Reorder them by argument strength. Explain the new order.

15_min.title: Priority-Weighted Essay
15_min.instruction: Write a 4-paragraph essay where paragraph word count reflects argument importance (strongest: 100w, second: 80w, counter: 70w).

micro_skills.definition: Move between levels of generality — from specific cases to general principles, and from general principles back to specific applications — to build and verify arguments.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: No direct writing skill covers this. Abstraction is the cognitive operation behind both argument construction (specific → general claim) and example integration (general claim → specific illustration).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who cannot abstract from examples to principles produce data dumps. Writers who cannot instantiate principles with examples produce empty claims.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: (a) does the writer state general principles from specific cases? (b) does the writer correctly instantiate general claims with specific examples?

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of identifiable specific→general or general→specific moves in essay
Proportion of abstraction moves that are logically valid

strength_indicators: Essay moves between specific evidence and general principles
Warrant sentences operate at higher abstraction than claim sentences
failure_indicators: Essay stays entirely at example level — no general principles stated
Essay states only abstract principles with no concrete instantiation

2_min.title: Specific → General
2_min.instruction: Take 3 specific examples. Write a general principle that each illustrates.
2_min.success_criterion: All 3 are genuinely more general than examples

5_min.title: General → Specific
5_min.instruction: Take 3 abstract claims. Write a specific example that instantiates each.
5_min.success_criterion: All 3 correctly instantiate the abstract claim

10_min.title: Abstraction Ladder
10_min.instruction: Write a 4-sentence sequence: specific case → principle → broader principle → application.
10_min.success_criterion: Each step moves correctly up or applies the ladder

15_min.title: Abstraction-Aware Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Write a paragraph that explicitly moves from specific evidence to a general principle and back to a specific prediction.

micro_skills.definition: Combine multiple distinct arguments, evidence items, or perspectives into a unified, higher-order conclusion that is not merely a summary.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: conclusion_construction (D5) measures whether a conclusion paragraph is present and well-formed. Synthesis is the thinking operation behind it: can the writer derive a genuinely new insight from combining their arguments?
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A conclusion that merely lists what was argued is a summary. A synthesis produces a statement that follows from the combination of arguments — this is the Band 7+ conclusion marker.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: does the conclusion state something that could not have been stated in the introduction without first making all the body arguments? If yes — genuine synthesis.

evaluation_metrics.description: Conclusion contains a proposition not present in introduction or any single body paragraph
Synthesis references at least 2 distinct body arguments

strength_indicators: Conclusion makes a claim that combines insights from multiple paragraphs
Conclusion could not have been written before the body was written
failure_indicators: Conclusion is identical to introduction
Conclusion summarises each paragraph sequentially
Conclusion states a position already visible in the introduction

2_min.title: Novel Conclusion
2_min.instruction: Given 2 arguments, write a conclusion sentence that is true only if BOTH arguments are true.
2_min.success_criterion: Sentence is logically dependent on both arguments

5_min.title: Introduction vs. Conclusion Comparison
5_min.instruction: Read your last essay's introduction and conclusion. Are they identical in content? If yes, write a synthesising conclusion instead.
5_min.success_criterion: Conclusion either confirmed as novel or rewritten

10_min.title: Argument Combination
10_min.instruction: Write a synthesis conclusion that derives a new implication from combining your 2 main arguments.

15_min.title: Full Synthesis Essay
15_min.instruction: Write an essay where the conclusion articulates an insight that emerges specifically from the interaction of both body arguments.

micro_skills.definition: Identify feedback loops, interdependencies, and systemic effects of a change — understanding that interventions in complex systems produce non-linear and sometimes paradoxical outcomes.
micro_skills.how_it_differs_from_writing_skill: No existing skill covers this. Systems thinking is a meta-cognitive competence — understanding that a policy change affects multiple subsystems simultaneously, often with competing effects.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: High-scoring academic writing at C1+ level demonstrates awareness of systemic complexity. Simple linear cause-effect arguments are flagged as shallow by university markers.
micro_skills.measurement_from_essays: Measured by: (a) does the writer identify interdependencies between systems? (b) are feedback effects or paradoxical consequences mentioned? (c) is complexity acknowledged?

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of sentences acknowledging interaction between two systems or stakeholder groups
Writer acknowledges that outcomes depend on context or interact with other variables

strength_indicators: Essay mentions how one system's change affects another system
Complexity and context-dependence acknowledged in conclusions
failure_indicators: All arguments are linear single-cause single-effect
No acknowledgment that outcomes depend on other variables
Policy proposed as if it operates in isolation

2_min.title: Identify the Interaction
2_min.instruction: For a given policy, name 2 systems it affects and 1 way they interact.

5_min.title: Feedback Loop
5_min.instruction: Describe a feedback loop for a given topic: action → effect → how the effect influences the original action.

10_min.title: Systemic Complexity Paragraph
10_min.instruction: Write a paragraph acknowledging 2 systemic interdependencies of a proposed solution.

15_min.title: Systems-Aware Essay
15_min.instruction: Write a problem-solution essay where the solution section explicitly acknowledges 2 systemic interactions and qualifies the solution accordingly.

micro_skills.definition: Select words whose semantic range is narrow enough to convey precisely the intended meaning without ambiguity from over-broad alternatives.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET flags over-broad words (affect, impact, people, thing, issue) and proposes a ranked list of semantically narrower alternatives with usage examples.
positive_evidence_indicators: Content words belong to specific semantic fields
Agent nouns are specific (researchers / educators vs. 'people')
Verbs are precise (surge / plummet vs. 'change')
negative_evidence_indicators: Repeated use of all-purpose nouns: thing, aspect, issue, factor, people
Vague verbs: affect, happen, occur used without more specific alternatives

evaluation_metrics.description: Count of all-purpose nouns (thing, aspect, factor, issue, area, people, society) per 100 words
Mean semantic narrowness of content word choices (evaluated against lexical range database)

strength_indicators: Content words from specific semantic fields
Vague noun density < 2 per 100 words
failure_indicators: ≥ 5 vague nouns per 100 words
Same all-purpose word repeated across every paragraph

2_min.title: Vague Word Hunt
2_min.instruction: Underline all vague nouns and verbs in a given paragraph.

5_min.title: Specificity Upgrade
5_min.instruction: Replace 5 vague words with more specific alternatives. Context-check each.
5_min.success_criterion: All replacements more specific and contextually accurate

10_min.title: Specificity Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for vague nouns and verbs. List all. Replace the most frequent.

15_min.title: Zero-Vague Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last body paragraph with zero all-purpose nouns. Verify.

micro_skills.definition: Use words whose full denotative meaning matches the intended proposition — avoiding near-synonyms that carry slightly different meanings which distort the claim.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET flags semantic inaccuracies by checking the full semantic field of each content word against the proposition it appears in. Suggests replacements with semantic field analysis.
positive_evidence_indicators: Word choice preserves exact propositional content across paragraph
Near-synonyms are used only when their meaning differences are irrelevant
negative_evidence_indicators: 'Raise' used for 'increase' where direction is ambiguous
'Affect' used for 'damage' or 'improve' — losing evaluative dimension
'Result in' vs. 'contribute to' misused — changing causal strength

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of word choices that create a meaning distortion

strength_indicators: Near-synonym selection never changes the propositional content
Evaluative dimension of words preserved (harmful vs. different)
failure_indicators: Paraphrase changes the strength of a causal claim
Synonym substitution turns a negative into a neutral

2_min.title: Meaning Shift Detector
2_min.instruction: Identify which of 4 word substitutions changes the meaning of a sentence.

5_min.title: Semantic Field Map
5_min.instruction: For the word 'increase', list 5 alternatives and map each: same meaning / stronger / weaker / directional change.

10_min.title: Paraphrase Accuracy Check
10_min.instruction: Paraphrase 3 sentences. After each, verify that the paraphrase has identical propositional content.

15_min.title: Precision Paraphrase
15_min.instruction: Paraphrase your introduction without changing any propositional content. Flag any word where you had to accept a slight meaning shift.

micro_skills.definition: Produce multi-word expressions that sound natural to a proficient speaker — not just grammatically possible but conventionally established through usage.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET flags unusual multi-word combinations, ranks them by frequency in academic corpora, and proposes natural replacements with frequency data.
positive_evidence_indicators: Multi-word expressions appear in academic writing corpora at high frequency
Preposition-noun-verb combinations follow established patterns
negative_evidence_indicators: Word combinations that are grammatically valid but never used by native speakers
'make a contribution to improve' (should be 'contribute to improving')
Literalistic translation of L1 idioms

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of multi-word expressions rated as unnatural by corpus frequency check
Proportion of multi-word expressions rated as natural by corpus check

strength_indicators: Multi-word units match established academic phrasing
Zero literalistic L1 constructions detectable
failure_indicators: Grammatically correct but unnatural phrasing every 2–3 sentences
Phrasal combinations that would never appear in an academic corpus

2_min.title: Natural or Constructed?
2_min.instruction: Label 6 multi-word expressions as natural or constructed.

5_min.title: Phrase Repair
5_min.instruction: Replace 4 unnatural phrases with natural alternatives.

10_min.title: Naturalness Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for unnatural phrases. List them with natural alternatives.

15_min.title: Natural Phrase Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last body paragraph ensuring every multi-word unit is corpus-natural.

micro_skills.definition: Ensure that the verb (predicate) is semantically compatible with its subject and object (arguments) — that the verb can apply to the entity named by the subject, and that the entity named by the object can receive the action.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET checks verb-subject and verb-object compatibility using selectional restriction rules. Flags violations and proposes compatible alternatives.
positive_evidence_indicators: Abstractions are not used as agents of physical actions without nominalization
Inanimate objects are not given agentive verbs unless metaphor is clearly intended
negative_evidence_indicators: 'The economy breathes new life into...' (economy as agent of animate action)
'Technology witnesses rapid growth' (inanimate agent of perception verb)
'The data argues that...' (data cannot argue)

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of verb-subject or verb-object selectional restriction violations

strength_indicators: All predicate-argument combinations are semantically valid
Metaphorical incompatibilities are either avoided or clearly intentional
failure_indicators: Abstract nouns used as agents of physical or perceptual verbs without metaphor framing
Inanimate objects 'argue', 'believe', 'witness'

2_min.title: Compatibility Check
2_min.instruction: Identify which of 4 sentences has a verb-subject incompatibility.

5_min.title: Repair the Violation
5_min.instruction: Rewrite 3 sentences with predicate-argument violations.

10_min.title: Verb Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay's verbs. Check each verb-subject pair for compatibility.

15_min.title: Compatible Verb Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph replacing all agentive verbs applied to abstractions with compatible constructions.

micro_skills.definition: Produce vocabulary and phrasing that is specifically appropriate to academic written discourse — including use of nominalization, impersonality, hedging, and academic sentence patterns — as distinct from merely formal language.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET tracks academic vocabulary list membership (AWL), nominalization frequency, passive use rate, and hedging marker frequency to produce a composite academic register score.
positive_evidence_indicators: AWL words used correctly and frequently
Nominalization used to create information-dense noun phrases
Passive voice used for agentless or process-focused statements
negative_evidence_indicators: First-person narrative in academic context
Dynamic action verbs dominate where nominalization is expected
Conversational hedges ('I think', 'maybe', 'kind of') in place of academic hedges

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of content words from Academic Word List
Nominalized verb forms per 100 words
Academic hedging markers used correctly vs. informally

strength_indicators: AWL coverage ≥ 0.12 (12% of content words)
Nominalization used for key analytical terms
Hedging in academic form: it is argued / evidence suggests
failure_indicators: AWL coverage < 0.05
Virtually no nominalization in academic essay
'I think' and 'I believe' as hedges in formal academic writing

2_min.title: AWL Recognition
2_min.instruction: Identify which 6 of 10 given words are on the Academic Word List.

5_min.title: Nominalization Upgrade
5_min.instruction: Convert 4 verbal sentences to nominalized equivalents.

10_min.title: Academic Register Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay: AWL density, nominalization count, academic hedge form.

15_min.title: Academic Register Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last essay's introduction improving AWL density, nominalization, and academic hedging.

micro_skills.definition: Convert verbal or adjectival concepts into noun phrases to achieve lexical compaction, informational density, and academic register — while avoiding over-nominalization that creates unreadable noun stacking.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET detects nominalization opportunities (verbs that have established noun forms in academic use) and flags over-nominalization (3+ noun modifiers before head noun).
positive_evidence_indicators: Verbal concepts expressed as nouns when followed by analysis: 'the implementation of...' vs. 'they implemented'
Nominalization creates thematic cohesion across paragraph
negative_evidence_indicators: 3+ pre-noun modifiers creating impenetrable noun stacks
Nominalization used in simple sentences where verb is clearer
'The undertaking of the conducting of the analysis' type stacking

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of nominalizations that are grammatically and contextually correct
Number of noun phrases with 3+ pre-nominal modifiers

strength_indicators: Nominalizations are accurate and improve information density
Noun stacks ≤ 2 pre-nominal modifiers
failure_indicators: Nominalization form is incorrect (employ → *employal)
Nominalization creates ambiguity about who performed the action
Noun stacks of 3+ modifiers obscure meaning

2_min.title: Nominalize It
2_min.instruction: Convert 5 verbal phrases to their correct nominalized forms.

5_min.title: Stack Reduction
5_min.instruction: Reduce 3 over-nominalized noun stacks by restructuring the phrase.
5_min.success_criterion: All stacks reduced to ≤ 2 modifiers; meaning preserved

10_min.title: Nominalization Audit
10_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for both under-nominalization and over-nominalization.

15_min.title: Nominalization-Balanced Paragraph
15_min.instruction: Rewrite a paragraph to achieve optimal nominalization: dense but readable.
15_min.success_criterion: Both metrics improved; evaluator confirms readability

micro_skills.definition: Produce paraphrases that simultaneously change lexical form, syntactic structure, and information focus while preserving the exact propositional content of the source.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET evaluates paraphrase quality on 3 dimensions: lexical change ratio, structural change, and meaning preservation. Provides graded feedback per dimension.
positive_evidence_indicators: Lexical change ratio ≥ 0.7 for content words
Syntactic structure differs from source
Propositional content identical to source
negative_evidence_indicators: Only 1–2 synonym substitutions with no structural change
Paraphrase reverses meaning by changing a negation
Paraphrase adds information not in source

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of content words changed from source
Syntactic structure is different from source
Propositional content preserved without distortion

strength_indicators: All 3 dimensions achieved simultaneously
Structural change uses a fundamentally different syntactic pattern (active→passive, verb→nominalization)
failure_indicators: Structural change without lexical change (same words rearranged)
Lexical change without structural change (synonym substitution only)
Both changed but meaning distorted

2_min.title: 3D Paraphrase
2_min.instruction: Paraphrase one sentence hitting all 3 dimensions: change ≥ 65% of content words, change structure, preserve meaning.

5_min.title: Structural Transformation
5_min.instruction: Paraphrase 3 sentences using a structural transformation each: active→passive, verb→nominalization, direct→reported.
5_min.success_criterion: All 3 use stated transformation; meaning preserved

10_min.title: Prompt Paraphrase Quality Check
10_min.instruction: Paraphrase your last essay's prompt introduction. Rate it on all 3 dimensions. Improve the weakest.

15_min.title: Paraphrase the Essay
15_min.instruction: Paraphrase your introduction twice using different transformation strategies each time. Compare quality.

micro_skills.definition: Maintain lexical variety across an essay by systematically varying how key concepts are expressed — using synonyms, near-synonyms, pronouns, nominalized forms, and superordinates — while avoiding both repetition and imprecise substitution.
micro_skills.lret_integration_strategy: LRET tracks lexical chains for key concepts across the essay. Identifies where the same word is overused and proposes contextually appropriate variation options ranked by semantic accuracy.
positive_evidence_indicators: Key concepts expressed with varied vocabulary across paragraphs
Type-token ratio ≥ 0.50 for academic essays
Variation strategies include: synonym, hypernym, nominalization — not just synonym
negative_evidence_indicators: Same content word appears > 3 times in 250 words
Variation achieved only by synonym substitution — structural variation absent
Type-token ratio < 0.40

evaluation_metrics.description: Type-token ratio normalised for essay length (target ≥ 0.50)
Number of distinct variation strategies used: synonym / hypernym / pronoun / nominalization / ellipsis

strength_indicators: 3+ distinct variation strategies used across essay
Type-token ratio ≥ 0.50
No content word repeated > 3 times in 250 words
failure_indicators: Type-token ratio < 0.40
Only synonym substitution used for variation
Same word in same sentence or adjacent sentences

2_min.title: Variation Strategies
2_min.instruction: For the word 'problem', produce 1 variation of each type: synonym / hypernym / pronoun reference / nominalization.

5_min.title: Lexical Chain Audit
5_min.instruction: Choose one key word from your last essay. Trace every occurrence. Replace overused instances with varied forms.
5_min.success_criterion: No word appears > 3 times; all replacements accurate

10_min.title: Type-Token Check
10_min.instruction: Calculate the type-token ratio for your last essay. Identify the 5 most repeated content words. Replace 3.

15_min.title: High-Variation Rewrite
15_min.instruction: Rewrite your last body paragraph using all 4 variation strategies at least once.

micro_skills.definition: Identify grammatical errors in one's own or others' writing systematically by category (agreement, tense, article, preposition, form, structure) rather than by chance.
micro_skills.differs_from_d10: D10 grammar_error_detection is the general ability. This skill is the systematic, category-driven process with a specific checklist methodology.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Detection without methodology produces inconsistent results. Writers who check systematically by category detect significantly more errors than writers who read for general sense.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of grammar error categories systematically checked
Proportion of actual grammar errors found in a seeded revision task

strength_indicators: Detects ≥ 80% of seeded errors in category-by-category scan
Detects errors across all 6+ categories
failure_indicators: Detection rate < 50% even with a checklist
Only catches surface-level errors (spelling) while missing structural ones

2_min.title: Single Category Scan
2_min.instruction: Scan your last paragraph for article errors only. List all found.

5_min.title: 6-Category Checklist
5_min.instruction: Apply the grammar checklist (articles, agreement, tense, preposition, form, punctuation) to one paragraph.
5_min.success_criterion: All 6 categories checked; all errors found

10_min.title: Full Essay Scan
10_min.instruction: Apply 6-category checklist to your full last essay. Calculate per-category error counts.
10_min.success_criterion: All categories checked; all errors found

15_min.title: Seeded Essay Detection
15_min.instruction: An essay with 10 planted grammar errors (2 per category across 5 categories) is provided. Find all 10.

micro_skills.definition: Identify lexical problems systematically across 5 categories: precision errors, collocation errors, register violations, word form errors, and spelling errors.
micro_skills.differs_from_d10: D10 lexical_error_detection is the general ability. This skill is the structured, category-driven process.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Lexical errors are more varied than grammar errors and require category-specific sensitivity. Systematic detection is the prerequisite for targeted repair.

evaluation_metrics.description: Number of lexical error categories systematically checked
Proportion of actual lexical errors found in a seeded revision task

strength_indicators: Detects ≥ 75% of seeded lexical errors across all 5 categories
Can distinguish precision error from collocation error
failure_indicators: Only spelling errors found — deeper lexical errors missed
Collocation errors consistently missed

2_min.title: Collocation Scan
2_min.instruction: Scan one paragraph for collocations. Mark any that feel unnatural.

5_min.title: 5-Category Lexical Check
5_min.instruction: Apply the lexical checklist (precision, collocation, register, form, spelling) to one paragraph.

10_min.title: Full Lexical Audit
10_min.instruction: Apply 5-category checklist to your full last essay.
10_min.success_criterion: All categories checked; all errors found

15_min.title: Seeded Lexical Detection
15_min.instruction: Find 10 planted lexical errors (2 per category) in a provided essay.

micro_skills.definition: Identify sentences or passages that require re-reading to comprehend, distinguishing clarity failures from grammatical correctness.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: A sentence can be grammatically correct but unclear. Clarity problems are frequently invisible to the writer because they know the intended meaning.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of unclear sentences identified in a seeded clarity audit task
Proportion of clear sentences flagged as unclear (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Detects ≥ 80% of unclear sentences without flagging clear ones
Can name the specific cause of each clarity problem
failure_indicators: Unclear sentences missed because writer fills in meaning from context
Over-flagging: marks complex but clear sentences as unclear

2_min.title: Re-Read Test
2_min.instruction: Read 5 sentences. Mark any you had to re-read.

5_min.title: First-Time Reader Test
5_min.instruction: Cover your last essay. Read it as if seeing it for the first time. Mark every unclear sentence.

10_min.title: Clarity Audit + Cause
10_min.instruction: Find unclear sentences in your last essay. For each, name the cause: ambiguous reference / pronoun / overloaded sentence / jargon.
10_min.success_criterion: All unclear sentences found; causes named

15_min.title: Clarity Detection + Fix
15_min.instruction: Audit your last essay for clarity. Rewrite all sentences rated unclear.
15_min.success_criterion: All unclear sentences found and fixed

micro_skills.definition: Identify cohesion failures systematically: misused transitions, ambiguous reference, broken information flow, over-linking, and missing inter-paragraph links.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: CC problems are the least self-detectable writing problems because cohesion operates below the level of individual sentence meaning.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of cohesion problems found in a seeded CC audit task
Number of CC problem categories systematically checked: transitions / reference / flow / overlinking / inter-paragraph

strength_indicators: Detects ≥ 75% of CC problems across all 5 categories
Can distinguish over-linking from appropriate transition use
failure_indicators: Only misused transitions detected — other CC problems missed
Overlinking never detected

2_min.title: Transition Audit
2_min.instruction: Scan one paragraph. Identify any misused transitions.

5_min.title: 5-Category CC Check
5_min.instruction: Apply CC checklist (transitions / reference / flow / overlinking / inter-paragraph links) to one paragraph.

10_min.title: Full CC Audit
10_min.instruction: Apply CC checklist to your full last essay.

15_min.title: Seeded CC Detection
15_min.instruction: Find 10 CC problems (2 per category) in a provided essay.

micro_skills.definition: Determine the underlying cause of a detected error — distinguishing surface symptoms from root causes — so that repair addresses the source rather than the instance.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Fixing symptoms produces recurring errors. Identifying root causes (e.g., 'I always use the wrong article with abstract nouns' vs. 'I made an error here') enables targeted skill remediation.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of detected errors where root cause is correctly identified
Writer distinguishes between the surface error and the underlying skill gap

strength_indicators: Can name the specific rule or principle being violated for each error
Can distinguish 'I made a slip' from 'I don't know this rule'
failure_indicators: All errors attributed to carelessness
Same error type recurring with no pattern recognition
Error named by surface form ('article error') not by cause ('article with uncountable nouns')

2_min.title: Error → Rule
2_min.instruction: For 3 given errors, name the rule being violated.

5_min.title: Pattern Recognition
5_min.instruction: Look at your last 3 essays' error logs. Identify the top 2 recurring error types.

10_min.title: Error Taxonomy
10_min.instruction: Classify your errors from last essay into: knowledge gap / rule confusion / attention slip. Count each type.

15_min.title: Root Cause Report
15_min.instruction: Write a 100-word self-analysis: what are your top 3 root causes of errors and what skill does each indicate needs work?
15_min.success_criterion: Root causes correctly identified; skill recommendations accurate

micro_skills.definition: Accurately assess the impact of a detected error on the reader's comprehension and on the essay's score — distinguishing high-severity errors (meaning loss, band impact) from low-severity errors (minor inelegance).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Writers who treat all errors equally waste revision time on low-severity issues while leaving high-severity problems unfixed. Severity estimation enables efficient triage.

evaluation_metrics.description: Correlation between writer's severity rating and evaluator's severity rating for same errors

strength_indicators: Severity ratings match evaluator within 1 level on a 3-point scale
High-severity errors consistently rated higher than low-severity ones
failure_indicators: Treats spelling errors as equally severe as argument gaps
Rates all errors as high-severity (over-anxiety)
Rates all errors as low-severity (under-awareness)

2_min.title: Severity Sort
2_min.instruction: Rate 5 errors as high / medium / low severity. Justify each.

5_min.title: Impact Analysis
5_min.instruction: For 3 errors, write: 'If left uncorrected, this error would cause the reader to...'

10_min.title: Error Triage
10_min.instruction: List all errors in your last essay. Rank them by severity. Compare your ranking to an evaluator's.

15_min.title: Severity-Priority Revision
15_min.instruction: Revise your last essay fixing only the top 5 severity-ranked errors. Verify your rankings were correct post-fix.

micro_skills.definition: Order the repairs to be made during revision by their expected impact on overall writing quality, allocating available time to highest-gain interventions first.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Under exam conditions, writers have limited revision time. Prioritization skill ensures that the highest-impact repairs are completed first.

evaluation_metrics.description: Mean score improvement per minute of revision time spent (higher is better)
Correlation between repair order chosen and optimal repair order by impact

strength_indicators: Always fixes TR/content issues before LR/GRA issues
Spelling never prioritised over argument gaps
Revision improves score per minute at high rate
failure_indicators: Fixes spelling first while leaving argument gaps unfixed
Same amount of time on all error types regardless of severity

2_min.title: Priority Ranking
2_min.instruction: Given 6 errors, rank them in revision priority order. Justify top 3.

5_min.title: 5-Minute Revision Plan
5_min.instruction: Given a flawed essay, plan which 3 repairs to make in 5 minutes. Justify each.

10_min.title: Priority-First Revision
10_min.instruction: Revise your last essay in 10 minutes. Fix only the highest-impact items. Log what you fixed and why.
10_min.success_criterion: All fixes are high-impact; log justifications accurate

15_min.title: Timed Priority Revision
15_min.instruction: Full revision with a timer: set 15 min. Fix in strict priority order. After each fix, re-rank remaining.

micro_skills.definition: Successfully correct a detected grammar error by applying the correct rule, without introducing a new error in the surrounding text.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Detection without successful repair is worthless. Grammar repair requires knowing not just that an error exists but what the correct form is.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of detected grammar errors successfully corrected
Proportion of repairs that introduce a new error in adjacent text (lower is better)

strength_indicators: Repair success rate ≥ 0.85
Zero regression errors — repairs don't break surrounding text
failure_indicators: Fixes one error but introduces another in the same sentence
Repair attempt leaves ambiguity
Repair changes grammatical form but not the correct form

2_min.title: Repair 5 Grammar Errors
2_min.instruction: Correct 5 given grammar errors. Verify each repair is correct and doesn't introduce new errors.

5_min.title: Repair + Verify
5_min.instruction: Fix all grammar errors found in your last paragraph. Re-read each fix in context.

10_min.title: Category-Specific Repair
10_min.instruction: Fix all article errors in your last essay. Verify rule was applied correctly for each.

15_min.title: Full Grammar Repair Pass
15_min.instruction: Complete a full grammar repair pass on your last essay. Compare error counts before/after.
15_min.success_criterion: Error count reduced by ≥ 80%; no regressions

micro_skills.definition: Successfully replace a detected lexical error with an accurate, contextually appropriate, naturally collocating alternative that preserves the intended meaning.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Lexical repair is harder than grammar repair because there are multiple possible alternatives and the wrong choice may introduce a new error of a different type.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of detected lexical errors successfully corrected without introducing new errors
Repair preserves original intended meaning

strength_indicators: Repair improves precision without distorting meaning
Repair introduces a natural collocation, not just a dictionary synonym
failure_indicators: Synonym chosen that is less precise than the original
Repair fixes collocation but changes register
Meaning subtly shifted by word choice

2_min.title: Collocation Repair
2_min.instruction: Correct 4 collocation errors. Verify each with a natural usage check.

5_min.title: Precision Repair
5_min.instruction: Replace 3 imprecise words with more precise alternatives. Verify contextual fit.
5_min.success_criterion: All more precise; all contextually accurate

10_min.title: Full Lexical Repair Pass
10_min.instruction: Apply a lexical repair pass to your last essay. Fix all detected lexical errors.
10_min.success_criterion: All errors fixed; meaning preserved; no regressions

15_min.title: Before/After Lexical Comparison
15_min.instruction: Repair all lexical errors in your last essay. Calculate LR improvement using an error count comparison.

micro_skills.definition: Fix detected reasoning gaps by adding missing logical steps, strengthening weak reasons, and replacing invalid causal claims — without restructuring the entire paragraph.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Reasoning repair is the highest-leverage revision activity because it directly raises task response scores. It requires both detection and the ability to construct the missing logical element.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of detected reasoning gaps successfully filled with valid logical content
argument_step_depth after repair vs. before repair

strength_indicators: Repaired paragraphs have measurably higher reasoning depth
Added logical steps are valid and necessary (not padding)
failure_indicators: Gap 'filled' by adding more examples instead of reasoning
Added sentence restates existing content without advancing logic
Repair makes paragraph longer but not deeper

2_min.title: Add the Missing Reason
2_min.instruction: For 3 claim-example pairs with no reason, write the missing reason sentence.
2_min.success_criterion: All 3 logically connect claim to example

5_min.title: Shallow → Deep
5_min.instruction: Identify your last essay's shallowest argument. Add 2 logical steps to deepen it.

10_min.title: Reasoning Repair Pass
10_min.instruction: Apply a reasoning repair pass to your last essay. Add missing reason sentences to any unsupported claims.
10_min.success_criterion: All unsupported claims now have reason sentences

15_min.title: Full Argument Reconstruction
15_min.instruction: Take your weakest body paragraph. Reconstruct it from scratch using full C-R-S-E-L structure.
15_min.success_criterion: All 5 elements present; reasoning depth score ≥ 0.75

micro_skills.definition: Fix detected cohesion problems by correcting misused transitions, clarifying ambiguous references, improving information flow, and reducing over-linking.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Cohesion repair directly raises CC scores. It is one of the most time-efficient revision activities because CC errors are concentrated and individually addressable.

evaluation_metrics.description: Proportion of detected CC problems successfully fixed
Change in CC score after repair

strength_indicators: All detected CC problems fixed; none reintroduced
CC score measurably improves post-repair
failure_indicators: Transition fixed but information flow not addressed
Over-linking reduced but new over-linking introduced elsewhere

2_min.title: Fix 3 Transitions
2_min.instruction: Correct 3 misused discourse markers in a given paragraph.

5_min.title: Reference Repair
5_min.instruction: Fix all ambiguous pronoun references in your last essay.

10_min.title: CC Repair Pass
10_min.instruction: Apply a CC repair pass to your last essay fixing all 5 categories.
10_min.success_criterion: All CC problems fixed; no regressions

15_min.title: CC Before/After
15_min.instruction: Repair all CC problems in your last essay. Get CC scores before and after.
15_min.success_criterion: CC score improves; no new CC problems introduced

micro_skills.definition: Accurately predict the quality level of one's own essay across key dimensions — task response, coherence, lexical quality, grammatical range — before seeing an external evaluation.
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Accurate self-evaluation is the foundation of autonomous improvement. Writers who cannot evaluate their own work accurately cannot prioritise their learning or make independent progress.

evaluation_metrics.description: Correlation between self-rated quality and evaluator-rated quality (by dimension)
Systematic over- or under-estimation (categorical: over / calibrated / under)

strength_indicators: Self-ratings within 0.5 band of evaluator ratings consistently
Bias direction is calibrated, not systematic
failure_indicators: Consistently over-rates by > 1 band (overconfidence)
Consistently under-rates (anxiety-based inaccuracy)
Cannot distinguish strong from weak paragraphs in own work

2_min.title: Quick Self-Rate
2_min.instruction: Rate your last essay on a 1–9 scale for each of 4 criteria. Submit before seeing evaluator scores.

5_min.title: Paragraph Quality Sort
5_min.instruction: Rank your last essay's body paragraphs from strongest to weakest before seeing feedback.

10_min.title: Calibration Analysis
10_min.instruction: Compare your last 3 self-evaluations to evaluator scores. Calculate your bias direction and magnitude.

15_min.title: Calibrated Evaluation
15_min.instruction: Evaluate your essay using evaluator rubric criteria. Submit self-scores. Compare to evaluator. Identify systematic biases.

micro_skills.definition: Match one's expressed confidence in specific language choices to the actual likelihood that the choice is correct — neither overconfident (committing to wrong choices) nor underconfident (avoiding correct choices due to anxiety).
micro_skills.why_it_matters: Overconfident writers submit errors they know are wrong. Underconfident writers replace correct vocabulary and structures with weaker alternatives. Calibration maximises correct commitment.

evaluation_metrics.description: Correlation between writer's confidence in a choice and whether the choice is actually correct
Proportion of 'certain' choices that are incorrect

strength_indicators: High-confidence choices are correct ≥ 90% of the time
Uncertain choices are revised; confident choices are retained
failure_indicators: Confident about errors; uncertain about correct forms
Changes correct vocabulary choices during revision due to unfounded uncertainty

2_min.title: Confidence Rating
2_min.instruction: Rate confidence (high/medium/low) for 10 language choices. Then check each.

5_min.title: Uncertain Choice Audit
5_min.instruction: In your last essay, mark every choice you were uncertain about. Verify each.

10_min.title: Calibration Check
10_min.instruction: Compare confident vs. uncertain choices. Are you correctly uncertain about errors and confident about correct forms?

15_min.title: Calibration Training
15_min.instruction: Rate confidence for every language choice in your last essay. Check all. Calculate: high-confidence accuracy rate and low-confidence error rate.
15_min.success_criterion: High-confidence accuracy ≥ 80%; low-confidence error rate ≥ 50% (correctly identified uncertainty)

micro_skills.definition: Deploys passive constructions accurately and appropriately for academic register. Avoids over-use of active voice where passive is the academic default, and avoids incorrect passive formation.
Selects and uses modal verbs (may, might, could, should, must, will, would) with correct meaning and grammar to express hedging, obligation, possibility, and stance.
Coordinates grammatically parallel elements in lists, compound structures, and comparisons without breaking parallelism.

Selects the most significant overall trend or pattern from data and expresses it as a general overview statement (not specific figures). The overview is the single most IELTS Task 1-critical structural requirement.

Uses precise quantitative language to describe data: directional verbs (rose, fell, declined, peaked), degree adverbs (sharply, gradually, slightly), prepositions of range (from X to Y, by X%), and comparison structures.
Hedges data claims with appropriate approximation markers (approximately, around, roughly, just over, nearly, about) when precise figures are unavailable or being generalised.

phase_2.description: Extended argumentation, thinking, advanced lexical, revision

changes_from_v2: D10 removed (merged into D15 conceptually; D15 is the sole Revision domain)
D12 eliminated: 6 skills absorbed into D11, 3 migrated to D5, 1 migrated to D3
Added evaluator_accessible and primary_evaluation_bucket flags per skill
ID system: snake_case is canonical throughout
